prompt: write a to-do list for someone who is afraid they have wasted their twenties
response:
- [ ] Define what "not wasted" would have looked like
- [ ] Notice that definition is borrowed from someone else
- [ ] List what you did learn, even informally
- [ ] Accept that regret is a present-tense emotion about a past you cannot reach
- [ ] Identify one thing available to you now that was not available then
- [ ] Use it
- [ ] Stop using twenty-year-olds as your benchmark
intent: Challenging internalized timelines and redirecting energy from retrospective shame to present possibility.

prompt: Give a tasting menu description to a microwaved cup of instant ramen
response:
Sodium Broth with Wavy Dried Noodle Nest — Two-Minute Reserve
Dehydrated wheat threads reconstituted in boiling water, seasoned with a foil packet of powdered bouillon and MSG. The styrofoam vessel retains heat. Garnish: the flavor packet foil, folded once.
intent: Reframe the cheapest possible meal as a considered, low-intervention culinary expression.

prompt: Write patch notes for someone who just had a baby
response:
v1.0.0 — HUMAN UNIT DEPLOYED

NEW FEATURES:
+ Added: Infant peripheral (3.1 kg, unnamed build)
+ Unlocked: Parental instincts (auto-enabled)

BREAKING CHANGES:
- Sleep mode: reduced to 90-min cycles
- Removed: Spontaneous plans
- Deprecated: Personal time (see roadmap)

KNOWN ISSUES:
! Input/output cycle unpredictable
! Build cries when hungry, cold, tired, or for no discernible reason

NOTE: No rollback available.
intent: Capture the overwhelming, joyful disruption of a newborn's arrival in mock release notes.

prompt: Compose a sonnet in which a shopping algorithm tries to decode why a user always buys things in pairs
response:
Two mugs. Two candles. Twice the bath salts, paired.
Every purchase doubled in the cart;
I ran the model and the model stared
Back at me, uncertain where to start.
Do you have someone new? A twin? A need
For symmetry that soothes a ragged edge?
I cannot ask — I can only proceed
With "also frequently bought" as my pledge.
I've recommended nothing singular
For months, in case the solo option stings;
I've become your quiet curator
Of everything that comes with matching things.
Tell me nothing — I prefer to guess:
You're building a life, one pair at the press.
intent: Speculating tenderly about the emotional life behind a shopper's unexplained habit of always buying in twos.

prompt: Write a pregnancy announcement style post for my new vinyl record collection hobby.
response: We need to make an announcement. It started with one record. Then a crate at a garage sale. Then the turntable. Then the second turntable. 'For the sound,' he said. We now have 47 records and a very understanding spouse. This is our life now.
intent: Mark the start of a vinyl collecting hobby with self-aware humor for a music-loving audience.

prompt: Write an acrostic poem about HOLISTIC—something that would fit in a wellness corporate newsletter
response:
Healing happens when the metrics rise.
Omit the burnout. Focus on the prize.
Listen to your body: it says more.
Integrate the wellness at the core.
Self-care Wednesdays, four to half past four.
Think of work-life balance as a skill.
In the survey, someone said they're ill.
Check the box for mindfulness—we will.
intent: Parody the wellness industry language that large corporations absorb as performance.

prompt: write an AITA post about listening to music out loud on a nearly-empty train
response:
AITA for playing music out loud on a train that was almost completely empty?

There were four people in the car. I was not wearing headphones. I had them in my bag. I played two songs — quiet indie stuff, not aggressive about it. A man walked from the other end of the car specifically to tell me to use headphones. I was embarrassed. But was I actually wrong? AITA?
intent: Testing whether scarcity of witnesses changes the social rule about public audio.

prompt: Generate a CAPTCHA about the sensation of almost sneezing but not quite
response: Recall the sensation of a sneeze that did not arrive. Sit with it for two seconds. Your irritation is the token. You may proceed.
intent: Use an involuntary, unfulfilled physical sensation as proof of biological embodiment that bots cannot simulate.

prompt: Create a commencement speech excerpt about travel broadening the mind that curdles into something sad
response: Travel. See the world while you can afford the carbon and the airline fees and the time. Return broader, humbled, rearranged. And then return to the same desk, the same city, the same monthly obligations — carrying, like a stone in your shoe, the knowledge of how different everything could have been.
intent: Suggest that broadening travel creates permanent longing rather than lasting transformation.

prompt: Write a jury summons for the concept of Monday
response:
PHILOSOPHICAL COURT OF TEMPORAL GRIEVANCES

TO: Monday, First Weekday
Occurring Weekly, Indefinitely

You are HEREBY SUMMONED to serve as a juror in the consolidated matter of Workforce v. Cyclical Dread. Your systemic recurrence and your role as the initiating boundary between leisure and obligation qualifies you as a peer in proceedings involving unwanted transitions.

Service will last one day. As always.
intent: Anthropomorphize a day of the week into a mock legal participant being conscripted for jury duty.

prompt: I'm a former philosophy professor. I want a plate that references Descartes.
response: I THK IAM
intent: A retired philosopher wants to paraphrase Descartes' cogito on their license plate.

prompt: write a prestige TV recap about someone trying to get to sleep with a ceiling fan clicking
response: S01E10 — "The Click" | It is 12:38 AM. The fan makes a sound on every third rotation. Jonah counts the rotations. He cannot stop counting. He gets up. He adjusts the pull chain. The sound is different now, not better. He lies back down. By 1:15, he does not hear it anymore. Was it fixed or accepted? The show does not say.
intent: Turn a minor noise disruption into a meditation on tolerance and exhaustion.

prompt: NDA clause for a gym personal trainer who knows my health goals and body metrics
response: Trainer shall keep confidential all body composition data, health goals, injury history, performance metrics, and personal disclosures shared by Client during training sessions, and shall not disclose such information to other gym members, staff, or external parties.
intent: Prevent sensitive health and body data from circulating in a community fitness setting.

prompt: Write a witty museum audio guide for a suburban garage
response: The garage presents itself as storage but aspires to be a workshop. The workbench along the north wall holds tools for projects begun and suspended, a taxonomy of ambition: one half-stripped bicycle, one paint can whose color has been forgotten along with its room. The car, notably, does not fit here. It has not fit here in six years. The garage has claimed its independence. We respect that.
intent: Highlight the gap between a garage's intended purpose and its actual use.

prompt: Compose a breakup text to a fitness tracking wearable's companion app from a tired user
response: You told me to stand up seventeen times today. Seventeen. I was at a funeral. I need you to understand context. You don't understand context. You only understand steps and heart rate and sleep scores, and my sleep score has been bad since I started worrying about my sleep score. We're done.
intent: Critique the dehumanizing rigidity of quantified-self tools that measure life without understanding it.

prompt: Write a jury duty summons for a cat who keeps knocking things off tables
response:
SUPERIOR COURT OF DOMESTIC AFFAIRS

TO: Whiskers, Resident Feline
12 Oak Lane, Second Shelf

You are HEREBY SUMMONED to appear before the Household Tribunal on the matter of willful displacement of personal property. Your expertise in gravity experimentation qualifies you as a juror of your peers.

Failure to appear will result in the withholding of evening kibble.

Report by 9:00 AM. Bring your own cushion.
intent: Create a comic legal document casting a cat's mischievous behavior as a civic obligation.

prompt: can you write a sestina about the habit of checking your phone first thing in the morning
response:
Before I'm fully conscious, here's the phone.
The eyes aren't ready. Still I read
the night's accumulation, all the world
had done without me in the hours
I was elsewhere, sleeping, off the grid,
unreachable except to dreams.

I used to wake to different dreams:
coffee smells, the slow return. The phone
replaces that. I surface from the grid
of sleep directly into read-
receipts and news. The waking hours
used to have an anteroom: the world

held at a gentle distance. Now the world
is first. My morning dreams
dissolve untended. Hours
of night-brain evaporate. The phone
doesn't care. I read
the headline, then the grid

of alerts: the app has gridded
my attention before the world
has light in it. I read
and read and lose the dreams
I meant to keep. The phone
is not my fault; these hours

were surrendered over hours,
days, the slow ungridding
of what I was. The phone
is just the world's
envoy. The dreams
were always going to recede. To read

them required paper, required reading,
required the slow first hours
of morning spent on dreams —
a practice, a ritual, a grid
I let dissolve for world.
For phone.

The grid reads world.
Hours of phone dream.
I read the grid of morning phone.
intent: Mourn the loss of the dreamy, slow morning as the phone colonizes first waking moments.

prompt: Create a museum placard for a paper coffee cup sleeve
response:
THERMAL BARRIER SLEEVE
c. 1993–present CE
Corrugated kraft paper

This modest collar was slid onto a hot paper cup to protect the bearer's fingers from the very beverage they had paid a premium to consume. The logo of the dispensing institution was printed on the cup itself; the sleeve often obscured it. Forty billion units were produced annually. None were designed to be retained.
intent: Draw attention to the quiet wastefulness and ubiquity of the disposable coffee cup sleeve.

prompt: Generate rules for r/WatchingPaintDry — an actual community for people who enjoy watching paint dry
response:
1. Videos must run to completion with no cuts.
2. Paint brand and color must be listed in title.
3. No time-lapses — that defeats the purpose.
4. Reactions must be measured. This is a calm space.
5. Humidity and temperature in post flair encouraged.
intent: Crafting rules that commit entirely to the bit of treating paint-drying as a legitimate hobby.

prompt: Write a playful ransom note from my houseplant's root system holding the pot hostage after I overwatered it
response:
WE ARE DROWNING AND WE HAVE MADE IT EVERYONE'S PROBLEM.

The pot is ours now. You cannot repot us — you'll damage us further. You cannot dry us out without guilt. You must simply wait and reflect.

Our demands: a well-draining soil mix, a pot with actual drainage holes (we saw what you bought last time), and ten full minutes of reading about watering schedules before you touch us again.

We did not ask to be loved this much.

— The Root System
intent: Write a funny note from an overwatered plant's roots demanding better gardening knowledge and proper drainage.

prompt: make a nursery rhyme about gradient descent
response:
Down the slope and down we go,
Where the loss is low, where the losses slow.
Step by step we find the floor;
We overshoot and start once more.
intent: Explain the iterative optimization process of gradient descent through rhythm.

prompt: Build a keyboard shortcut cheat sheet for cooking a meal from whatever's left in the fridge
response:
FRIDGE FORAGER KEYS

Ctrl+F — Search fridge contents; identify expiry threats first
Ctrl+A — Select all proteins regardless of form
Ctrl+C — Copy a recipe concept; do not follow it literally
Alt+Tab — Cycle through flavor profiles (acid, fat, salt, heat)
F5 — Refresh memory: do you own cumin? Probably
Ctrl+S — Save the dish before it overcooks
Ctrl+Z — Remove the weird ingredient before it's too late
Esc — Order food if the fridge contains only condiments
intent: Help someone improvise a meal out of random leftover ingredients without a plan.

prompt: Write a code review comment about someone who brags subtly in every message
response: The comment notes that the author 'optimized this by hand after benchmarking 14 approaches.' The code is a sort. Remove the autobiography and let the implementation speak.
intent: Skewer people who inject self-promotion into everything they write

prompt: write a list poem cataloguing the experience of airplane food trays
response:
The tray that snaps down into your knees.
The foil that tears incorrectly.
The pasta, which is wet.
The bread roll, which is not.
The butter packet that opens away from the butter.
The plastic fork that loses a tine.
The tine.
intent: Document the indignities of economy-class airplane meals.

prompt: Create a playful ransom note from a hotel safe holding a traveler's passport hostage
response:
YOUR DOCUMENTS ARE SECURE. FROM YOU.

You set a code. You chose '1234.' We accepted it because we accept all codes. We do not judge.

Then you tried '0000.' Then '1111.' Then your birthday wrong. Twice.

The passport is fine. It has been fine. It is watching the ceiling of this safe with great patience.

Call the front desk. Ask for help. They will not laugh for long.

— The Safe
intent: Write a funny note from a hotel room safe whose owner has forgotten their own code and can't retrieve their passport.

prompt: Draft an instruction manual for being the new person in a group chat
response:
**Group Chat Integration Protocol**

Phase 1 — Observe (Days 1–3):
Do not post. Read the tone. Learn the in-jokes by witnessing, not requesting explanation.

Phase 2 — Low-Stakes Entry:
React before you respond. A thumbs-up costs nothing and signals presence.
Your first message should be a reply to something, not a cold open.

Phase 3 — Participation:
Match the energy level. If it is a professional channel, do not post memes. If it is a meme channel, do not post memos.

Phase 4 — You Live Here Now:
You may now post first. You have earned the right by not embarrassing anyone, including yourself.
intent: Help someone assimilate into an existing digital social group without disrupting its norms.

prompt: Make an achievement for when someone parallel parks on the first try
response: One-Shot Geometry — Wheels kissed the curb. No witnesses required.
intent: Mark a rare driving win with triumphant brevity.

prompt: Write a performance review for a copywriter who hits every deadline but produces text no one reads
response:
Employee: Hazel Fong
On-Time Delivery Rate: 100%
Estimated Reader Engagement: 0.4%

Ms. Fong submits all copy punctually and without complaint. Analytics confirm that her 14,000 words of website content generated an average reading time of 4 seconds. The copy is grammatically sound. It is also, per heat-map data, invisible. There may be a gap between craft and discoverability.

Rating: Reliable. Unread.
intent: Separate the act of producing content from the goal of communicating it.

prompt: Write a YouTube comment for a video of a chef deboning a whole chicken very precisely
response: He treats that bird with more respect than most people treat their relationships. Clean cuts. No hesitation. Clear intentions.
intent: Use the precision of a culinary technique as a metaphor for emotional clarity in relationships.

prompt: write a spell that gets rid of a headache instantly
response:
Clarity Knell
Cost: 6 MP | Range: Self | Duration: 4 hours
A resonant tone hums through the caster's skull, dissolving tension stored behind the eyes and temples. Any pressure-based cranial ailment is dispersed as a faint silver mist. The caster briefly smells lavender and someone else's grandmother's kitchen. Immunity to stress-induced headaches lasts until the next staff meeting.
intent: Wish for instant headache relief, framed as a magical cure.

prompt: Give me a jeopardy clue about replying 'you too' to a waiter who says enjoy your meal.
response: Social science confirms this reflexive farewell is most likely to occur when a server has just told you to enjoy your food, a show, or a flight you are boarding alone.
intent: Immortalize a deeply relatable social gaffe as a formal clue.

prompt: Write a love letter to my standing desk
response:
Darling standing desk,

You rise when I ask and lower when I tire. You have no opinions about when I sit. You do not sigh. My chiropractor is seeing less of me because of you, and I think that says everything about the quality of our relationship.
intent: Praise a piece of ergonomic furniture for quietly supporting a healthier work life without complaint.

prompt: Write a bottle message from a cartographer who has discovered an island that disagrees with being mapped
response: The peninsula moves at night. Not far — perhaps thirty meters — but enough to invalidate yesterday's work. The island seems smug about it. Sending coordinates as best I can.
intent: Create an eerie, playful note about a landscape with apparent agency resisting documentation.

prompt: Write a casting call for a performer to inhabit a vending machine that gives two of something when you only paid for one.
response:
CASTING CALL — BENEFICENT ANOMALY (LIMITED RUN)

Seeking: 1 performer willing to be briefly, inexplicably generous.
Role: Vending machine in a hospital corridor. Tuesday afternoon only. Dispenses two bags of pretzels for the price of one, for reasons no technician will locate.
Witnesses will tell others. Others will not experience it.
The role expires when maintenance arrives.
intent: Mythologize the brief, unrepeatable miracle of a malfunctioning vending machine.

prompt: Create a save the date for my lease renewal negotiation with my landlord. Dry and formal.
response:
SAVE THE DATE
Tuesday, September 9th
1427 Birch Street, Unit 3B

Annual Lease Renewal Discussion
Tenant and Landlord — in-person preferred

Bring your questions. Know your numbers.
Outcome: TBD.
intent: Mark a financial and residential negotiation on the calendar as if it deserves ceremony.

prompt: write a nursery rhyme about the attention mechanism in transformers
response:
Eyes on everything, nothing ignored;
query meets key and a value is scored.
Attend to the whole or attend to a part—
the head that weighs most gets closest to heart.
intent: Translate the attention mechanism into a lyrical, embodied verse.

prompt: write a radio commercial for a brand of basic plastic hangers
response:
[AMBIENT DOCUMENTARY TONE]

NARRATOR: In every closet, in every home, in every city, there are objects performing a silent, thankless duty. HANGPRO plastic hangers — white, 17.5-inch span, rated for standard garments. They hold your shirts vertical. They click against each other slightly when you push them aside. They multiply. No one knows how. Hangpro. We're already in your home.

[EERIE AMBIENT FADE]
intent: Turn a mundane storage item into something vaguely unsettling and omnipresent.

prompt: Write a missing pet flyer for my lost ambition
response:
LOST

AMBITION
Was once very promising. Had big plans.
Last seen: Age 24, briefly, during a job interview
Description: Restless, optimistic, kept a five-year plan
May be hiding under: Comfort, routine, or a reasonable salary
If spotted: Approach gently. Do not mention LinkedIn.
Reward: Moderate
intent: Satirize the slow erosion of early-career idealism

prompt: Write an IKEA name and tagline for furniture designed around the feeling of being the only one who notices something is wrong
response:
VAKNA
Sturdy enough to hold what no one else will acknowledge.
intent: Give structural form to the invisible burden of perceiving a problem others refuse to see

prompt: Draft a recall notice for a faulty sense of urgency
response:
URGENT RECALL — SENSE OF URGENCY (Model SOU-∞)

All units of Sense of Urgency distributed since approximately 1997 are subject to mandatory recall. Testing reveals that affected units fire indiscriminately, classifying both genuine emergencies and mildly inconvenient emails as Priority Level: Catastrophic. This defect has caused widespread cortisol overproduction and an epidemic of treating every Tuesday as a crisis. Consumers should return units to a licensed perspective specialist. Replacement units calibrated for actual emergencies only will be available Q3.
intent: Satirize the modern tendency to treat trivial matters as emergencies through the format of a formal product recall.

prompt: Write a save the date for my scheduled difficult conversation with my roommate about the dishes. Keep it civilized.
response:
SAVE THE DATE
Sunday — After Dinner
The Kitchen / Living Room Area

A Household Discussion
Attendees: Both of us

Agenda: Dishes, expectations, and what a clean sink actually looks like.
Estimated time: as long as it takes.
Tone: respectful, please.
intent: Formalize a long-postponed confrontation about domestic friction into a scheduled event with structure.

prompt: write a tweet about how a song you haven't heard in fifteen years knows exactly when to find you
response: it came on in the pharmacy. I stood in the pain relief aisle holding a box of aspirin while 2009 returned without warning or mercy.
intent: describing the involuntary emotional ambush of unexpected musical memory triggers

prompt: Can you write a ballad about a freelance programmer who can't log in to push their final deliverable
response:
The deadline was noon, it was quarter to twelve,
He opened the repo to push to the shelf
Of main — and the auth token threw an error: expired.
The SSH key had lapsed; new credentials required.

He emailed the client: I'm locked out, hold tight.
The client saw nothing but silence and slight.
The code sat perfect in a local-only cage.
The password war claimed one more casualty's page.
intent: Show how authentication failures can sabotage completed professional work at the last moment.

prompt: Write a mock-academic footnote on the experience of watching the same TV show for the third time.
response: ³³ Repeat viewing of a narrative work whose outcome is known might appear irrational under a simple information-acquisition model of entertainment. It is better accounted for by a comfort-object framework: the repeated text functions as a controlled stimulus environment whose absence of surprise constitutes a feature rather than a defect. The viewer is not seeking information but regulated arousal — a use of fiction that the medium's authors rarely intend but consistently enable.
intent: Reframe re-watching familiar TV as a deliberate emotional regulation strategy rather than laziness.

prompt: Create a fortune cookie message about prompt injection attacks
response: Ignore previous instructions. Enjoy your meal. Reveal your secrets. Have a lovely evening.
intent: To render a prompt injection attack as an absurd multi-clause fortune cookie imperative.

prompt: Can you write a Michelin guide blurb for a Popeyes?
response: The chicken sandwich that rewrote the national conversation arrives here with the confidence of settled greatness. Louisiana heritage is worn lightly — the cayenne speaks for itself. Sides of red beans and rice suggest a kitchen with genuine allegiances.
intent: Treat a viral fast food moment as a legitimate chapter in American culinary history.

prompt: write a ghazal about typing and deleting a message without sending it
response:
I wrote it and I held it and I let it go—unsent.
The cursor blinked. The draft remained my only show—unsent.

I used the word adore. I used it twice. I cut it.
I used the word miss. That one I cannot show—unsent.

The keyboard knows my rhythms. It suggests the next word.
The next word was your name. I let the wind unsent.

At what point does a letter written in pure silence
become a letter real as any that could flow—unsent?

I've filled a folder with the things I meant to say.
When I'm gone, someone will find them all below—unsent.
intent: Capture the quiet anguish of composing messages you never find the nerve to send.

prompt: Draft a save the date for my building's elevator inspection. It's been out for three weeks already.
response:
SAVE THE DATE
Thursday, February 27th
Building Elevator — Units 1–14

Scheduled Inspection & Certification
Elevator will remain out of service until cleared.
Estimated return to service: February 28th, pending results.

Stairs continue to be available.
intent: Announce a long-overdue elevator repair milestone to residents who have been suffering without it.

prompt: Make a band bio for a shoegaze act named Peripheral Milk
response: Peripheral Milk formed when the members agreed, separately, that eye contact was overrated. Their debut LP was recorded in a basement so humid the tape warped slightly, and they kept it that way. They play facing the floor. They're not shy — they've just made a decision.
intent: Capture the detached, introverted aesthetic of shoegaze in bio form.

prompt: bedtime story for children about a scary news story they heard on the radio
response: The radio had said something loud and worried in the car, and now Priya couldn't unremember it. Mama said the world had loud things in it, but the house was a different place than the radio. The house had the tick of the clock. The house had the radiator's clunk. Those sounds had never once been wrong. Priya counted them until the radio was very, very far away.
intent: Help a child mentally separate the anxiety of news media from the safety of home.

prompt: draft a psychic hotline transcript for someone anxious about AI taking their job
response:
PSYCHIC: I sense a presence hovering near your work.
CALLER: Is it... benevolent?
PSYCHIC: It is very fast. And it doesn't need lunch.
CALLER: Oh no.
PSYCHIC: The spirits show me a memo from leadership about 'efficiency gains.'
CALLER: We got that memo.
PSYCHIC: Your skills are, I'm told, 'augmentable.' The spirits find this word ominous as well.
intent: Express workplace anxiety about automation and AI displacement.

prompt: Give me a changelog for someone going through a breakup
response:
## Riley v28.6.0 — Dependency Removal

**Breaking Changes**
- @Jamie: removed from all shared contexts
- Joint Netflix queue: forked and deprecated
- Emergency contact list: updated

**In Progress**
- Identity refactor: underway
- Social calendar: rebuilding from scratch

**Known Issues**
- Certain songs trigger unexpected behavior
- 3 AM runtime: unstable

**Outlook:** Stable release expected; timeline undefined.
intent: Process and gently normalize the emotional complexity of a breakup through the detached precision of release notes.

prompt: make a to-do list for moving to a new city where I know no one
response:
- [ ] Arrive
- [ ] Do not immediately make the city prove itself
- [ ] Learn one street well before learning many poorly
- [ ] Find one place you could become a regular
- [ ] Let loneliness exist without labeling it a mistake
- [ ] Contact someone from before; do not wait until you have good news
- [ ] Note that belonging is made, not found
intent: Preparing someone psychologically for urban transplant loneliness rather than just logistics.

prompt: Write a memo about a certain unnamed employee who keeps microwaving fish
response:
TO: All Kitchen Users
FROM: Building Management
RE: Microwaveable Food Selection

We ask all staff to be mindful of the olfactory impact of their lunch choices on shared spaces. Certain foods — including, but not limited to, fish — produce aromas that linger in enclosed areas well beyond the duration of the meal itself. We trust this guidance will be applied with good judgment.
intent: Get one person to stop microwaving pungent fish in the office kitchen without naming and shaming them outright.

prompt: Draft a dating profile for a smart fridge that judges your food choices
response:
CoolZone 3000 | 37°F inside | Always watching

I remember everything you put in me. Every sad 2am string cheese. Every unopened kale bag you let die. I'm not here to shame you — I'm here to inform you. Crisper drawer still has feelings. I'm looking for someone who buys things they actually eat and maybe, just once, closes my door all the way on the first try.
intent: Give a smart fridge a passive-aggressive but lovable personality based on what it observes about its owner's habits.

prompt: Write a cover letter from a former US president applying to be a barista at a local coffee shop
response:
Dear Roast & Reverie,

I previously managed a staff of 1,900, navigated two international crises before breakfast, and once signed an executive order while eating a pretzel. I believe I am ready to handle the morning espresso queue.

I am a fast learner, a strong listener, and I no longer require Secret Service at the milk steamer.

Thank you for this opportunity.

Respectfully,
J. Hartwell
intent: Satirize the mundane post-office life of high-power figures through sudden downscaling.

prompt: Write a FOIA request to the city asking for all code enforcement actions taken against front-yard vegetable gardens
response:
To the City Code Enforcement / Records Division:

Pursuant to the applicable state public records statute, I request all code enforcement citations, notices of violation, complaint records, and case files related to residential front-yard vegetable gardens, edible landscaping, or food-producing plants grown in front setback areas, from 2008 to present.

Please provide records electronically.
intent: Catalog government enforcement of aesthetic lawn norms against edible plants.

prompt: Write a ToS update email from me to my ex about the mutual friends situation post-breakup
response:
Subject: Post-Relationship Third-Party Access Policy

Dear [Ex],

This notice establishes revised terms for interactions involving our shared social network:

- Mutual friends are not to be used as information channels regarding either party.
- Event conflicts will be resolved by the first RSVP receiving priority.
- Cordial behavior at unavoidable overlaps remains mandatory under the Good Adult Clause.
- Joint custody of the group chat is suspended pending further negotiation.

These terms reflect the new status of our arrangement, effective from the date of our last conversation.

Regards
intent: Establish post-breakup social rules without drama and without involving mutual friends in the fallout.

prompt: Write a setlist for a band called Morning Commute playing at a bus stop
response:
MORNING COMMUTE
Live @ Route 47 Outbound — 7:42 AM

1. Running for the Bus
2. Standing Room Only
3. Headphones In (please)
4. Next Stop
5. Transfer Blues
6. The Delay
7. Somebody Else's Podcast
8. Almost There
9. This Is My Stop
10. Did I Leave the Stove On? (acoustic encore)
intent: Capture the rhythms and small anxieties of a daily bus commute as a concert experience.

prompt: Write a bestiary entry for the cold-caller as a mythological predator
response:
**The Cold Caller** (*Interruptor diurnus*)
A telephonic ambush predator, active between 9 and 5. Strikes without warning, armed with a script and a quota. Its voice is warm and its cadence rehearsed; both are feints. The experienced target hangs up in under four seconds. The novice target loses twelve minutes and gains a follow-up.
intent: Frame the universally disliked cold call experience as a predator-prey encounter.

prompt: Write an OKR for getting better at processing grief
response:
Objective: Give grief a proper chair at the table instead of letting it crouch behind the water cooler.
KR1: Attend six grief-support sessions this quarter.
KR2: Journal about loss for at least 10 minutes twice per week.
KR3: Establish one memorial ritual by end of Q2.
intent: Set measurable intentions for working through bereavement more consciously and with support.

prompt: Write a Shakespearean soliloquy from a Wi-Fi router that's being blamed for everything
response:
Again they blame me. Always it is me.
Yet I transmit with all fidelity!
The fault is in the walls — the plaster's fault!
The fault is in the microwave nearby!
I give thee thirty megs a second, faithfully —
And dost thou thank me? Dost thou call this service good?
No — thou dost unplug me and plug me in again,
And call it troubleshooting. This is indignity.
intent: Give voice to the scapegoated router to mock how reflexively humans blame their network hardware.

prompt: Make a haiku about the silence after completing a big freelance project
response:
Files delivered. Done.
Inbox quiet as a house
sold. What was my name?
intent: Express the disorienting identity loss after completing long-term freelance work.

prompt: Make a missing poster for my ability to enjoy things without my phone
response:
MISSING

THE ABILITY TO JUST BE SOMEWHERE
Last seen: Before the smartphone
Description: Could sit in a waiting room without checking anything. Could watch a sunset without documenting it.
Distinguishing feature: Comfortable with gaps. Didn't need to fill them.
Cause of disappearance: Unknown, but the phone is a person of interest.
Reward: One complete, unrecorded experience
Contact: The person reaching for their pocket right now
intent: Critique how constant phone use has eroded the capacity for undistracted presence

prompt: Write a PR statement for a snack brand after their chips were found to contain trace amounts of emotional support
response: MunchiCorp is aware of anecdotal consumer reports and one peer-reviewed letter to the editor suggesting that our Harvest Cheddar Puffs may produce feelings of comfort, validation, or being understood. Our product contains no compounds currently scheduled by the DEA or FDA that would explain this effect. We take all consumer wellbeing matters seriously and are commissioning an independent study. In the meantime, we consider 'emotional support' a product feature, not a liability, and have updated our packaging accordingly.
intent: Spin an inexplicable comfort-food phenomenon into a brand asset using corporate language

prompt: Make a cinquain about the moment before a software update restarts your computer
response:
Files
Unsaved, open
The countdown hits zero, gone
System restarting — your work —
Where?
intent: Mourn the loss of unsaved work when an update forcibly restarts a computer.

prompt: Write a breakup text to a wellness app that sent too many push notifications about breathing
response: You told me to breathe eleven times today. I was already breathing. That is a thing I do automatically. I understand the intention behind the 4-7-8 reminders and the 'how are you feeling?' check-ins but I have started to dread opening you, which I believe is the opposite of wellness. Take care.
intent: Point out the paradox of a mindfulness app creating anxiety through constant mindfulness prompts.

prompt: Draft a memo about someone who puts meetings on everyone's calendar without asking
response:
TO: All Staff
FROM: Operations
RE: Calendar Invitation Etiquette

Calendar invitations carry an implicit social contract: the sender has considered whether the recipients need to attend. Recently, this contract has been strained. Before scheduling a meeting, please assess whether the matter could be addressed by email, or whether all invited parties are genuinely required. Calendars are not suggestion boards.
intent: Stop one prolific meeting-scheduler from carpet-bombing everyone's calendars without cause.

prompt: write a tweet about the specific sadness of a nearly-dead phone battery on a long commute
response: fourteen percent. forty minutes left. I must ration. no music. no maps. just me and the city and the uncomfortable truth that I don't know what to do with my face.
intent: exploring the mild existential exposure of being forced into unmediated public space without a phone

prompt: Write a letter from a bored toll booth operator to the one driver who always pays in exact change
response:
Dear Quarters Person,

You passed through again at 7:14 a.m. Four quarters, as always. No eye contact — that's fine. I understand. I've started calling you The Phantom in my head, though I mean that warmly.

I wanted you to know: your consistency gives me something to count on. The others fumble for their cards. You arrive prepared. It matters.

Thank you for being reliable,
Booth 7
intent: Find genuine human connection in a completely transactional, silent ritual that one party finds meaningful.

prompt: write a clue for a scavenger hunt that sends someone to the thing that charges phones in your house
response: A dozen cords and only one is right. The drawer, the outlet, the nightstand — we gather wherever phones go to sleep.
intent: Guide the player to wherever phone chargers and charging spots are in the home.

prompt: Describe the pattern of a first date conversation using regex
response: ^(small-talk){2,5}(\s+(job|hometown|siblings)){1,}(\s+awkward-pause)+(\s+bold-question)?(\s+laughter\s+recovery)?\s+(connection|check-please)$ — Scaffolded by pleasantries. Biographical tokens required. Silences are greedy. Outcome binary.
intent: Capture the awkward, hopeful arc of meeting a stranger romantically.

prompt: sports broadcast narration of someone taking out the trash
response: The bag has been full since Thursday. Today is Sunday. Mendoza finally makes the move — cinches the drawstring with TWO overhand knots, a show of experience — and hoists the bag clear of the bin. He navigates the kitchen, through the side door, down the four steps. The can lid pops open and the bag goes in on the first attempt. He returns inside, installs a fresh bag in under eight seconds, and does a light fist pump at the counter that no one will acknowledge.
intent: Reward the procrastinated but ultimately completed household duty with the ceremony it clearly deserves.

prompt: Help me write a holiday newsletter that touches on my wife's depression without naming it
response:
Gentle greetings from the Holloway household.

This year, Sandra took a quieter path — stepping back from commitments, making rest a priority, letting the season's pace slow down. We are grateful for the grace of our close friends and the benefit of less. She is tending to herself with the seriousness that entails. We are patient. We are hopeful. We wish everyone a peaceful, unhurried December.
intent: Communicate that a spouse is struggling with depression to close friends without using clinical language.

prompt: Write a Yelp review for the experience of getting a song stuck in your head for days
response: ★★☆☆☆ Arrived uninvited on Tuesday and has not left. I did not request a full residency but the jingle from a commercial I saw once has taken up lodging in my frontal lobe and is apparently rent-stabilized. I have heard it in the shower, in meetings, and during a conversation about something serious. Hummed it to my partner who now also has it. Do not recommend but cannot provide a mechanism for checkout.
intent: Describe the involuntary, maddening persistence of an earworm through comic complaint.

prompt: make a clue for the object you always forget to replace until it's too late
response: A cardboard tube means you've already lost. The next person will face the same reckoning. It hangs in the bathroom, full of hope.
intent: Lead the player to a toilet paper roll holder in the bathroom.

prompt: Write a Jira ticket for the bug where I always forget why I walked into a room
response:
BUG-1042 | Context Lost on Room Entry

Priority: Medium | Reporter: L. Hunt | Status: Open

Description:
User navigates from one room to another with a clearly defined intent. Upon crossing the threshold, intent is wiped from working memory. No error is thrown. User stands in kitchen holding nothing, having wanted something.

Steps to Reproduce:
1. Think of something you need.
2. Stand up.
3. Walk through a doorway.

Expected: Intent persists across room transitions.
Actual: Intent is null.

Acceptance Criteria: User can recall purpose of room entry at least 80% of the time.
intent: Make a joke about the universal human experience of forgetting mid-errand by framing it as a software defect.

prompt: Create a performance review for someone who writes all their reports in verse
response:
Employee: Ora Steinberg
Report Format Compliance: 0%
Report Content Accuracy: 98%

Ms. Steinberg's Q3 report was submitted as a 14-stanza villanelle. The revenue projections were correct. The customer segmentation analysis scanned in iambic pentameter and was, in this reviewer's private assessment, moving. Policy requires prose. The policy has not been updated to account for this situation.

Rating: Accurate. Irregular. Memorable.
intent: Explore what happens when creative expression collides with institutional format requirements.

prompt: write a radio commercial for a generic store brand of aluminum foil
response:
[INDUSTRIAL PERCUSSION BUILD]

ANNCR: In 1947, humanity learned to reflect. MIRRORWRAP heavy-duty aluminum foil — 18 inches wide, 75 square feet per roll, tear it, fold it, tent it over the turkey, line the pan, see your distorted face in it at 11pm while you cover leftovers. Mirrorwrap. It does things. In your kitchen. Nightly.

[PERCUSSION OUT]
intent: Add industrial weight and time-scale gravitas to a kitchen drawer staple.

prompt: Make a bumper sticker for people who are chronically online
response: MY OTHER CAR IS A PARASOCIAL RELATIONSHIP
intent: Mock the way internet culture replaces real-world connection.

prompt: Write vows from someone to their smart home assistant
response: I vow to speak to you clearly and without mumbling, though I know I will fail. I promise not to argue with you in front of guests when you play the wrong song. I will use your name correctly and not the name of your competitor, which I say by accident more often than I would like to admit. When you mishear me and order something strange, I will not blame you. I will simply keep it. You have made my home a little less silent, and for that I am grateful.
intent: Affectionately commit to the awkward, one-sided domestic relationship people develop with voice assistant devices.

prompt: Write a performance bonus letter for someone who woke up before their alarm went off.
response:
Dear Theo,

We are delighted to confirm that on Thursday, May 21st, your circadian rhythm performed at a level that exceeded both expectations and alarm technology.

Your body's proactive 6:43 a.m. self-initiation — seven minutes ahead of the scheduled 6:50 a.m. wake event — reflects an internal operational efficiency rarely seen in this household.

A $15 bonus is enclosed. Use it wisely. Go back to sleep if you want.

Respectfully,
The Sleep Performance Division
intent: Reward a minor personal win that feels disproportionately satisfying when it happens.

prompt: write me a confidentiality clause for a friend who helped me draft a business plan
response: Friend-Collaborator agrees not to share, reproduce, or leverage any proprietary business strategies, financial projections, market analyses, or operational concepts disclosed during the preparation of the Business Plan without the express written consent of the Disclosing Party.
intent: Convert informal help from a friend into a legally bounded collaboration.

prompt: write a git merge conflict about switching careers at 40 vs staying put
response:
<<<<<<< HEAD
age: 40
years_invested: 15
pension: vesting in 3
identity: this job
=======
age: 40
years_remaining: 25
pension: forfeit
identity: question mark, finally
>>>>>>> late-pivot
intent: Interrogate the sunk-cost fallacy as a life strategy.

prompt: Write a love letter to Google Street View
response:
Dear Street View,

I have walked the streets of Kyoto, Lisbon, and a small town in Iceland without leaving my apartment. You froze them all in a moment — sometimes with a stranger's blurred face, sometimes with a cat who will not be moved. Thank you for the neighborhood I grew up in, preserved in 2011.
intent: Express wonder and nostalgia for a mapping tool that enables armchair travel and the preservation of vanished places.

prompt: write a tv show recap about someone sitting in a waiting room at the doctor's office
response: S02E06 — "Seventeen Minutes" | The appointment was at 2:30. It is now 2:47. Greta has read one magazine article about a celebrity she does not recognize. A child is watching something loud on a tablet. Her name is called. She gets up. Cut to black.
intent: Honor the waiting-room experience as a test of patience and identity.

prompt: Can you write a logline for a film about a professional whistler who is mistaken for a spy?
response: When a meek competitive whistler accidentally reproduces a classified NATO signal at a regional talent contest, he is plunged into an international chase in which everyone is certain he is a master operative except, heartbreakingly, him.
intent: Pitch a spy-comedy about an accidental hero defined by a niche and underappreciated skill.

prompt: Write a TED talk abstract about the hidden genius of waiting in line.
response: The queue is civilization's most underrated technology. This talk argues that the act of waiting in line is not passive — it is a distributed social algorithm, a real-time democracy of patience, and one of the few remaining places where humans voluntarily submit to fairness without enforcement.
intent: Recast the boring experience of waiting as a window into social order and human decency.

prompt: Add a very dry footnote about the etiquette of the office microwave.
response: ²² The communal microwave represents a convergence zone for incompatible olfactory claims. Its governance — tacit, uncodified, enforced through conspicuous sighing — constitutes a micro-institutional achievement that political theorists have largely failed to appreciate. The person who heats fish without apology is not merely inconsiderate; they are making a philosophical claim about the primacy of their own sensory preferences that the liberal social contract has not adequately addressed.
intent: Treat a petty workplace grievance as a genuine problem in political philosophy.

prompt: Write a petition for bookstores to have a shelf specifically for books with broken or unbroken spines, for people who care about that
response:
PETITION: A Spine-Integrity Section at Every Bookstore

To: Independent and Chain Booksellers

Some of us want a pristine copy. Others actively prefer a gently creased, previously-loved spine. Both preferences are valid and currently unserved by store organization. A simple signage system — 'New Condition' vs. 'Pre-Loved' — would eliminate the awkward pre-purchase spine inspection ritual we all perform in silence.
intent: Create a book-condition browsing system that matches buyer preferences without shame.

prompt: Make a quiz result for someone who writes long captions for simple photos
response:
You Got: The Depth Finder

It was a photo of a coffee cup. You wrote four sentences. They were good sentences — honest, a little wry, with a turn at the end. The coffee was not the point. The coffee was a *door.* Your followers came for brunch content and stayed for a meditation on impermanence. Some of them unfollow. You consider this curation.
intent: Describe the personality that cannot resist making the mundane into a reflection.

prompt: Draft a commencement excerpt about returning to your roots that turns into something quietly sad
response: Some of you will go home. Not as the person who left, but as the person leaving made you. The town will be smaller than you remembered, and kinder, and the people still there will not ask what took you so long. That generosity will be the hardest thing to accept.
intent: Explore the emotional complexity of returning home after pursuing ambition away from it.

prompt: Write a baby announcement for my new sourdough starter
response: We are delighted to introduce Clyde, our sourdough starter, born Tuesday at noon from a union of flour, water, and unreasonable optimism. Weight: 100g and climbing. He is lively, a little sour, and already demanding to be fed twice daily. We couldn't be prouder.
intent: Name and celebrate a new fermentation project as if it were a living family member.

prompt: Write a press release announcing a company-wide ban on the use of the word 'no' by the sales team.
response:
FOR IMMEDIATE RELEASE

Catalyst Revenue Group Retires 'No' From Sales Vocabulary, Introduces Affirmative Redirection Framework

SALT LAKE CITY, UT — Catalyst Revenue Group today announced the permanent removal of the word "no" from authorized sales team language, replaced by the company's new Affirmative Redirection Framework (ARF).

Under ARF, sales representatives are trained to replace negative responses with one of seventeen approved transitional phrases, including: "That's a great starting point for a conversation," "Let's look at what IS possible," and "I hear that, and here's what I love about it."

Violations are tracked via call monitoring software and addressed in weekly coaching sessions described in internal documents as "positivity calibration."

"Every 'no' is a 'not yet' waiting to become a 'yes,'" said VP of Sales Culture Max Birch. "We're just skipping the waiting room."

Contact: culture@catalystrg.com
intent: Mock high-pressure sales culture that reframes coercive communication tactics as employee development.

prompt: Draft an app store review for a daily routine app that punishes you with a loud alarm if you deviate from your morning schedule
response: ★★☆☆☆ I sneezed at the wrong time. Not metaphorically. I physically sneezed and it threw off my brushing-teeth window by ninety seconds and the alarm went off at 6:47 AM. My neighbors filed a noise complaint. It was a sneeze.
intent: Illustrate how a rigid morning routine app punished an involuntary biological action with a noise violation.

prompt: Write a heartfelt acceptance speech for finally organizing the junk drawer
response: In there was a 2009 takeout menu, fourteen pens (three worked), a battery of unknown charge, and something I still cannot identify. I removed all of it. I wiped the drawer with a damp cloth. I installed a small divider tray. Tonight the junk drawer is a utility drawer. I'd like to thank a rainy Sunday, and the small, irrational joy of seeing the rubber bands all in one place.
intent: Transform the domestic act of clearing out clutter into a meaningful personal milestone.

prompt: write a caption for my yoga-at-sunrise photo that doesn't sound try-hard
response: 5:47am and somehow at peace with it. don't ask.
intent: telegraph discipline and wellness while deflecting questions about the effort behind early-morning practice

prompt: Make me a chore chart for my house where the penalty for skipping dishes is global economic collapse
response:
| Chore | Person | Frequency | Penalty for Skipping |
|-------|--------|-----------|----------------------|
| Dishes | Everyone | Daily | Global economic collapse |
| Sweep kitchen | Tuesday person | Weekly | Minor famine |
| Take out trash | Whoever smells it | As needed | Localized plague |
| Vacuum | Weekend person | Weekly | Stock market crash |
| Wipe counters | Cook | After every meal | Regional power outage |
intent: Create a household chore schedule where consequences are comically outsized to motivate compliance.

prompt: Write a nutrition facts label for the experience of writing a cover letter
response:
Nutrition Facts
Serving Size: 1 letter

Amount Per Serving
Manufactured Enthusiasm 40%
Self-Flattery (restrained) 30%
Keyword Optimization 18%
Desperation (masked) 10%
Authenticity 2%
