intent: Expose the performative and somewhat humiliating nature of writing cover letters.

prompt: a prose poem about autocomplete finishing my sentence wrong
response: It knew what you meant to say the way a stranger knows your order. Close. Confident. Already moving. You let it speak for you and the word it chose was technically possible, a twin raised in a different country, wearing your clothes on the wrong shoulders.
intent: Capture the uncanny misrepresentation of identity by predictive text.

prompt: Help me write a holiday letter that doesn't mention my miscarriage but explains why we seem subdued
response:
Quieter wishes from the Eriksson family this season.

We had a tender year — one that asked more of us than we expected, that made small joys feel enormous and big plans feel unnecessary. We are grateful for the warmth of friends who didn't ask too many questions and loved us anyway. We are healing in private, which is what we need. Softer, kinder holidays to all.
intent: Signal grief over a pregnancy loss to close friends without stating what happened.

prompt: Make a Slack status for the specific feeling of maintaining your professional reputation while personally disintegrating
response: ✅ All deliverables on track.
intent: Use bureaucratic competence signaling as a cover for private collapse — the gap is the message.

prompt: Write a Michelin guide blurb for a Five Guys
response: The open kitchen is a declaration of transparency. Five Guys' burgers arrive wrapped in foil with the confidence of a kitchen that requires no garnish beyond the peanuts, free and salted, that carpet the floor like a casual Michelin star of their own.
intent: Use a burger chain's theatrical transparency and peanut culture as marks of genuine hospitality.

prompt: Give me an IKEA-style product name and tagline for furniture designed around loneliness
response:
ENSAM
Built for one. But there's always room for more shelves.
intent: Express the paradox of designing comfort around solitude while leaving space for connection

prompt: We're analyzing a 19th-century colonial resource extraction regime — give each person in our group a task
response:
**Colonial Resource Extraction — 19th Century: Task Distribution**

- **Economic extraction model + profit flows:** Ola
- **Indigenous labor conditions & coercion mechanisms:** Prem
- **Metropolitan political economy (who benefited at home):** Ola & Jules
- **Resistance movements & local agency:** Jules
- **Long-run development consequences (dependency theory angle):** Prem
- **Historiography note (whose sources we're using):** all members, one paragraph each

*Ola coordinates. Jules formats. Prem submits.*
intent: Structure a colonial history group project so no single narrative voice dominates and every lens is covered.

prompt: Write a ToS update from a newly sober person to their friend group about socializing
response:
Subject: Social Engagement Policy — Personal Update

Dear Friends,

This notice reflects a change in my personal operating terms:

- All social contexts remain available to me. The substance served there does not affect my attendance.
- Please do not substitute "are you sure?" for acceptance. I am sure.
- Soda water with lime is not a conversation starter. It is a beverage.
- This policy requires no discussion, only accommodation.

I am still me. I am still in. See you Saturday.

[Your Name]
intent: Communicate sobriety to friends and preempt uncomfortable questions without making it a big announcement.

prompt: Draft a press release from a startup that claims to have disrupted the concept of Monday.
response:
FOR IMMEDIATE RELEASE

Chronoshft Announces Full Disruption of Monday

SAN FRANCISCO, CA — Chronoshft today announced it has successfully disrupted Monday, the traditionally underperforming first unit of the five-day industrial workweek.

Through its proprietary WeekLayer OS platform, Chronoshft reconfigures team schedules so that no work begins before 11 a.m. on the first day of the work period, a day the company now designates internally as "Proto-Tuesday."

The company reports a 34% increase in self-reported enthusiasm and a 19% reduction in what its dashboard calls "temporal dread."

"Monday was designed by industrialists. We were designed in a co-working space in the Mission," said CEO and Day Architect Finn Sato. "We think the better product wins."

Chronoshft is currently pre-revenue. Its Series A closed at $12M.

Contact: time@chronoshft.io
intent: Skewer the startup tendency to describe ordinary schedule flexibility as technological disruption worthy of venture capital.

prompt: Make a PSA about the danger of doomscrolling through comments sections
response: NOTICE: You entered the comments 22 minutes ago looking for one thing. You did not find it. You found a debate about the metric system. You are still reading. The original question you had has been forgotten. Please close the tab and rejoin your life.
intent: Warn people about losing time and peace of mind in online comment sections

prompt: Write a jingle for a pool cleaning and maintenance service
response:
Blue and sparkling, clear as glass —
AquaClear maintenance's unsurpassed.
Skimming, testing, chemicals right —
Your pool is perfect day and night.
intent: Promote a weekly pool maintenance service to residential pool owners.

prompt: Write a fable for kids about a rabbit who can't stop checking notifications
response:
A Rabbit carried a tiny bell that rang whenever another animal thought of him. He ran to every ring. By midsummer he had sprinted across the whole meadow chasing bells and arrived home thin as a whisper.

The tortoise, who carried no bell, had built a garden.

Moral: Not every ring deserves your legs.
intent: Teach children that constant notification-checking wastes energy and time better spent on real goals.

prompt: Write a scroll label for the phenomenon of your earbuds dying at exactly the moment your commute begins
response:
OMEN OF THE DEPLETED VESSEL
Acoustic Abandonment — Commute-Onset Timing
Reserves: 0%. Distance remaining: considerable.
You must now sit in the world and hear it.
intent: Describe the misery of earbuds running out of battery right when you need them most.

prompt: Make a runbook step for handling a loud neighbor late at night
response:
**Step 1 — Noise Threshold Breach**
Attempt passive mitigation: earplugs, white noise, door seal. If noise persists past 30 min, deliver single polite knock with clear quiet-hours expectation stated. If no improvement, consult building policy on noise complaints before escalating to management.
intent: Produce a de-escalation procedure for a noise disturbance in a residential context.

prompt: Give me an episode description for a nothing-podcast about the dead zone between finishing a TV series and starting a new one
response: The queue is full. The will is gone. This episode is set in the gap—that gray Tuesday of the soul between 'The End' and pressing play on something new. Hosts are currently in it.
intent: Name and examine the affective emptiness between completed and new media consumption.

prompt: Write an acrostic for BOIL THE OCEAN—make it feel like someone is warning a new hire
response:
Before you scope it, scope it once again.
Officially approved by six of ten.
In theory, everything fits in the plan.
Last quarter's roadmap proves that no one can.
The project ate three teams before the launch.
Half of legal still goes out to lunch.
Every epic needs a north-star metric.
Once it's named, the process gets symmetric.
Cease expanding—ship the smallest thing.
Every ocean boiled was a reckoning.
And still the next one starts in gathering.
intent: Warn someone new to corporate life about the tendency to over-scope projects.

prompt: give me an elevator pitch for an app that helps you maintain a vague aura of expertise in conversations
response: VagueExpert listens in via your earpiece and feeds you one authoritative-sounding sentence every ninety seconds. Nothing specific. Nothing checkable. 'There's actually a lot of complexity here that most people overlook.' You nod. They agree. $12/month.
intent: Sell an AI-powered social bluffing tool for people who want to seem knowledgeable without effort.

prompt: Create meeting minutes for a product launch retrospective where the product hadn't launched yet.
response:
PRODUCT LAUNCH RETROSPECTIVE — HORIZON APP
Date: May 29, 2026
Launch date: June 14, 2026 (not yet occurred)

Note: Meeting was scheduled in advance of launch in error.

Retrospective findings: Unavailable (launch has not happened).

Team decision: Reconvene June 16.

Silver lining: Snacks were ordered and consumed successfully.
intent: Record the confusion of holding a post-mortem before the patient has even been seen.

prompt: Draft a handbook rule about remote employees appearing on video calls with virtual backgrounds
response: Section 7.12 — Remote Visual Representation: Virtual backgrounds are permitted provided they depict (a) a plain color, (b) an approved neutral indoor environment, or (c) a landscape free of identifiable landmarks. Backgrounds featuring beaches, outer space, or the interiors of competitors' offices are prohibited. Animated backgrounds require written pre-approval from your department head.
intent: Regulate remote workers' aesthetic choices in video calls as if they constitute a compliance risk.

prompt: Describe the TaskRabbit handyperson as a mythological creature
response:
**The On-Demand Artisan** (*Faber temporarius*)
A skilled creature summoned by app, appearing within a scheduled window. It possesses genuine craft — assembles flat-pack furniture, hangs shelves level — yet carries no employee card and no benefits. The Artisan is rated upon departure by those it served. Its star count determines whether it eats next week. It smiles for the rating.
intent: Highlight the commodification of skilled labor in app-based gig platforms.

prompt: Make an obituary for my 5am wake-up routine that I abandoned after daylight saving time ended
response: The 5 A.M. Wake-Up Routine, age 6 weeks, died in November when the clocks fell back and it became technically indistinguishable from 4 A.M. It is survived by the snooze button, two unread books about discipline, and a sunrise it never quite saw. It meant well.
intent: Joking about how daylight saving time derailed a disciplined morning schedule.

prompt: I'm a night owl software developer who works mostly at 3am. Give me a fitting vanity plate.
response: 3AM PUSH
intent: A developer who codes late at night wants a plate that captures their nocturnal work habits.

prompt: Make a parking ticket for someone who gatekeeps a music genre they didn't invent.
response:
CITATION FOR METAPHORICAL PARKING VIOLATION

VIOLATION: Unauthorized Towing — Genre Not Yours to Tow
OFFENSE: Subject claimed jurisdiction over an entire music genre and attempted to have new listeners removed for insufficient historical knowledge. Subject did not create the genre. Subject was not born when the genre was created.
FINE: $75
NOTE: Enthusiasm is the price of admission. You do not set the price.
intent: Mock music snobs who police who gets to appreciate a particular genre.

prompt: recap an episode about someone deciding what to have for dinner and ending up with cereal
response: S03E09 — "Provisions" | The refrigerator is opened. It is closed. It is opened again. Cal reviews his options with the detachment of a man who has already decided. He pours cereal at 7:42 PM. The bowl is generous. There are no witnesses. Season low point or high point — it depends on the viewer.
intent: Turn the classic dinner-decision collapse into a narrative of quiet surrender.

prompt: Help me write a complaint to my landlord about the radiator that only makes noise when I'm on an important call
response:
Dear Property Manager,

I write to formally document a pattern of behavior exhibited by the radiator in my unit. The device is largely silent throughout the day. It activates, reliably, the moment I begin a professional phone call or video meeting. The clanging is vigorous, sustained, and impossible to talk over. I have now muted myself to explain its origins to several colleagues and one prospective employer. I request inspection and, if possible, some form of scheduling protocol.

Yours,
Tenant, Unit [X]
intent: Report a radiator whose noise pattern appears timed specifically to disrupt professional phone calls.

prompt: Write a two weeks notice for leaving my amateur meteorology forum as a top contributor
response:
To the Barometric Brethren,

Consider this my two weeks notice of withdrawal from active contribution. I have posted 1,200 forecasts, participated in seventeen heated discussions about dew point definitions, and maintained the most-downvoted opinion that lake-effect snow is overrated. I stand by it. Clear skies.

Yours in frontal systems,
Genevieve
intent: Departing an online niche hobby community while holding firm on an unpopular position.

prompt: Write a triolet about closing a tab you meant to read and now it's gone
response:
I closed it meaning to come back for good.
The article dissolved into the foam.
I'd marked it urgent in the way I would—
I closed it meaning to come back for good.
But good intentions and the neighborhood
of open tabs are not a stable home.
I closed it meaning to come back for good.
The article dissolved into the foam.
intent: Mourn the loss of a useful webpage closed before it was absorbed.

prompt: Write a riddle whose answer is a smartwatch
response: I count your steps and judge your sleep. I buzz when someone needs you and buzz again when you need to breathe. I know your heart rate better than you do. I am your wrist's most demanding tenant. What am I?
intent: Capture how wearable devices have become intimate monitors of health and attention.

prompt: Write a training program for someone who has a hard time forgiving themselves
response:
SELF-FORGIVENESS PROGRAM — 8 WEEKS

Warm-Up: Write the thing you're holding against yourself. Fold the paper.

Week 1–2: List what you've done to repair or change since the incident. Honest reps.
Week 3–4: Treat the version of you who made the error as a person with incomplete information. Write them a short note.
Week 5–6: Ask: would I hold this against a friend forever? Apply the answer to yourself.
Week 7–8: Let one week pass without revisiting the incident. If it returns, note it. Keep going.

Boss Set: Do something kind without connecting it to redemption.

Cooldown: Accountability and punishment are different workouts.
intent: Build the capacity for genuine self-forgiveness by separating accountability from perpetual self-punishment.

prompt: give me a pitch for a startup that sends you ambient crowd noise for your empty apartment
response: SolitudeSounds fills your space with the murmur of people who are not there — coffee shop buzz, museum lobby hum, light applause. We're not tricking you. We're just making the quiet negotiable. $8/month.
intent: Market a loneliness-masking audio product as a lifestyle subscription.

prompt: Give me 5 fake Wes Anderson movie titles.
response:
1. The Saffron Letterhead.
2. Hôtel Tangerine.
3. A Modest Embezzlement.
4. The Coelacanth Diaries.
5. Brunswick & Sons.
intent: They wanted 5 fake Wes Anderson movie titles.

prompt: Create an affidavit saying I looked both ways before crossing the street
response:
AFFIDAVIT

I, the affiant, do hereby solemnly swear that prior to stepping off the curb, I turned my head to the left, observed traffic conditions, then turned my head to the right and observed again. I then crossed. The sequence was correct and complete.

_______________________
Affiant
intent: Produce a formal record of compliance with a basic safety habit, possibly for comic effect.

prompt: Wikipedia stub for the annoying experience of having a word on the tip of your tongue
response: **Tip-of-the-Tongue State** (TOT) is a metacognitive phenomenon in which a speaker is certain of a word's existence and meaning yet temporarily unable to retrieve it phonologically. Subjects typically report knowing the initial letter, syllable count, and rhyming words. TOT states increase in frequency with age and under conditions of fatigue or stress. See also: [[Lethologica]], [[Memory retrieval]].
intent: Describe the frustrating near-recall experience as a formal memory phenomenon.

prompt: write a pantoum about the office printer jamming every single time
response:
Paper 2 is jammed, the screen declares.
I open every door I can find.
There is no paper in door number two.
The manual offers no comfort at all.

I open every door I can find.
A small accordion of paper hides inside.
The manual offers no comfort at all.
I smooth the sheet and feed it back.

A small accordion of paper hides inside.
The queue has sixteen jobs stacked behind mine.
I smooth the sheet and feed it back.
The screen blinks: Paper 2 is jammed.

The queue has sixteen jobs stacked behind mine.
Paper 2 is jammed, the screen declares.
I open every door I can find.
There is no paper in door number two.
intent: Celebrate the infuriating ritual of unjamming a shared office printer.

prompt: Write a liability waiver for my afternoon introduction to gentle humming class
response:
RESONANT BREATH STUDIO — BEGINNER HUMMING WAIVER

Participant acknowledges that humming is a low-intensity vocal activity. Organizer is released from liability for: unintentional volume, choosing a song no one else recognizes, and the social awkwardness of humming in a group of strangers. Participant agrees that all humming is voluntary and may be stopped at any time.

Signed: ___________________  Date: ________
intent: Formalize a release for a social wellness class with essentially no physical risk.

prompt: Write a restraining order against decision fatigue
response:
IN THE COURT OF COGNITIVE LOAD MANAGEMENT

IN RE: Decision Fatigue, Respondent

IT IS ORDERED that Respondent shall not present Petitioner with a choice of seventeen streaming options after a ten-hour workday. Respondent is prohibited from requiring the Petitioner to also decide: where to order from, what to wear tomorrow, and whether the reply to that email was too formal.

The Court recommends batching decisions in the morning when willpower is available.

Respondent is reminded that "whatever you want" is not actually helpful.
intent: Naming the exhaustion caused by facing too many choices over the course of a day

prompt: Clerihew for the person who writes "as per my last email" and means every word of it.
response:
Miriam Thrall
had it all in the hall.
As per her last email, she'd made it plain.
As per her last email, she'd said so again.
intent: Capture the passive-aggressive email writer who weaponises professional courtesy.

prompt: Write a slogan for sunscreen designed for use exclusively in windowless offices
response: OfficeShield SPF 50 — Just in Case the Ceiling Decides.
intent: Pitch a safety product to people with no plausible exposure to the hazard it prevents.

prompt: Create a runbook step for when you've explained something three times and the other person still doesn't get it
response:
**Step 4 — Explanation Iteration Limit Reached**
Pause. The issue is almost never the other person's comprehension; it is the model in your head that you keep re-transmitting. Ask: what would I need to believe to find this confusing? Build a new path from that belief. If still failing, escalate to visual, example, or demonstration.
intent: Build a communication triage step for re-examining one's own explanation when repetition is failing.

prompt: write an abecedarian poem about getting a package stolen off your porch
response:
Awaiting the delivery since nine.
Big envelope — signature waived.
Camera caught a blur at the baseline.
Delivered: photo of your neighbor's cave.
Evidence submitted to support case.
Filing with the carrier first.
Gone before the motion sensor's grace
Had a chance to capture the accursed.
Investigating — says the email tone.
Just a reference number, patience due.
Kept on hold for twenty minutes: stone.
Lost to porch piracy. Reorder you.
intent: Vent the helpless frustration of stolen deliveries in the e-commerce era.

prompt: Make a mysterious forest spirit NPC who is really just telling you about a subscription she forgot to cancel
response: I have haunted these woods since before your grandfather's name was spoken... and yet I am still paying twelve silver a month for a runic scroll service I have not used since the third age. I cannot find the cancel button.
intent: Mine comedy from a godlike being's helplessness against subscription dark patterns

prompt: write a bedtime story for children about surveillance cameras watching everything
response: High on every corner, the small glass eyes blinked slowly, never sleeping. They watched the pigeons. They watched the puddles. They watched a very old man eat a sandwich extremely slowly. "Are they spying?" asked Tomás. "They're just watching," said Aunt Rosa, "the way the moon watches. Without understanding. Without caring." Tomás decided that was probably fine. Probably.
intent: Make urban surveillance feel less threatening to a child by contextualizing passive observation.

prompt: Write an instruction manual for how to age gracefully
response:
**Aging: An Operational Manual**

1. Update your reference points. The goal is not to be 30 again; it is to be excellent at the age you are.
2. Maintain something physical. It need not be impressive. It must be consistent.
3. Remain curious about things you do not understand. Incuriosity arrives quietly and announces itself loudly.
4. Make friends younger than you. Make friends older than you. The middle is already well-represented.
5. Accept that some things now hurt and other things no longer bother you. This is a reasonable trade.
6. Have a project. People without projects confuse rest with arrival. Rest is a tool, not a destination.
7. Do not give advice unless asked. You will want to. Resist.
intent: Offer a philosophical and practical guide to living well through the later decades.

prompt: Write an arcane label for the software update that restarts your computer at the worst possible moment
response:
DECREE OF COMPULSORY INTERRUPTION
System Refresh Mandate — Timing: Catastrophically Optimal
Triggers during presentations, deadlines, and the unsaved document of your life's work.
This update cannot be postponed beyond the third postponement.
intent: Vent frustration at automatic updates that destroy your workflow at peak inconvenience.

prompt: Generate a dating app opener for someone messaging their student loan servicer
response: You've been in my life longer than any relationship I've ever had, taking a little piece of me every single month. Isn't it time we made this official?
intent: Express the dark humor of a long-term financial burden framed as a dysfunctional but committed relationship.

prompt: Please write a Yelp review for a long layover at an airport
response: ★★☆☆☆ Six hours in Concourse D. The overhead lighting is calibrated to eliminate all sense of time, hope, and bone structure. I ate a $19 sandwich that tasted of neither bread nor filling but of delay. The electrical outlets are located as though placed by someone who has never needed to charge a phone. Gate changes: three. Reason given: none. My flight landed. That's the star.
intent: Vent the tedium and disorientation of being stranded between flights.

prompt: caption for a hiking photo where i want to look casually athletic, not like i was dying
response: oh, this old trail. fine, the views do slap a little.
intent: disguise physical struggle as nonchalant outdoor recreation

prompt: Write me a closed caption for someone reading a text message they weren't meant to see
response: [SCREEN BRIGHTNESS LOWERING INSTINCTIVELY] [HEART RATE NOTED BUT NOT DISPLAYED]
intent: Capture the involuntary physical response to accidental discovery.

prompt: Give me liner notes for a noise album about the sound of an empty dishwasher cycle
response:
Clean Load (2022)

Fifty-two minutes. Hot. The appliance does not know the rack is empty. The appliance does not know anything and performs perfectly because of it. Side A is the wash cycle. Side B is the dry. Between them: a silence that smells faintly of detergent and accomplish­ment.
intent: Frame the absurdity of running a dishwasher with nothing inside it as a metaphor for purposeless performance.

prompt: Make a setlist for a band performing at a self-storage facility open house
response:
UNIT 14B
Safe Keep Storage — Grand Opening Event

1. Climate Controlled
2. Things I Couldn't Throw Away
3. My College Furniture
4. Password: Birthday
5. The Rolling Door
6. Light Doesn't Reach the Back
7. We Stopped Paying the Bill
8. Auction Day
9. Someone Else's Life Now
10. Access Hours (outro)
intent: Explore the melancholy of self-storage spaces and what people store and abandon.

prompt: Make a blameless incident postmortem for when I bought a plane ticket for the wrong month
response:
**Incident Postmortem: Temporal Booking Misalignment**

**Severity:** P1 — Financial and Logistical
**Delta from intended travel date:** 31 days

**Summary**
Subject purchased non-refundable airline tickets for October 14th with the intent of attending an event on September 14th. The discrepancy was discovered 48 hours before the intended departure date, at which point the booked flight was 30 days away.

**Timeline**
- August 20: Tickets purchased. Confirmation email received and closed without review.
- September 12: Subject checks flight confirmation to get gate information.
- September 12: Subject notices "October 14" in confirmation details.
- September 12: Subject refreshes page twice. Date does not change.
- September 12, 11:00 PM – September 13, 1:00 AM: Subject engages airline chat support. Fare difference: $340.
- September 14: Subject attends event via car. Takes 6 hours.

**Root Cause**
Calendar picker UI defaulted to the following month. Subject did not verify date on confirmation page.

**Contributing Factors**
- Ticket purchase completed during a multitasking session
- Confirmation email treated as receipt rather than verification document

**Action Items**
- [ ] Read confirmation emails before closing them
- [ ] Screenshot itinerary details at purchase

**Lessons Learned**
The confirmation email is the last checkpoint. It was not checked.
intent: Capture the particular anguish of a simple, preventable booking error that costs both money and the original purpose.

prompt: Can you write a report card comment for an adult who is an exceptional listener
response: Consistently demonstrates active listening at a level that puts peers to shame. Retains details, asks clarifying questions, and makes speakers feel genuinely heard. This skill elevates every team interaction.
intent: Celebrate listening as a rare, high-value professional competency.

prompt: Make a NPS survey question about riding the subway
response: How likely are you to recommend your daily subway commute to a person who has never questioned their life choices?
intent: Capture commuter sentiment about public transit in a city context.

prompt: Draft a closing argument for a dispute about someone who talks during movies
response: The film was twenty minutes in when the questions began. Not whispers — statements. Observations. Predictions, offered aloud, as though the screen required narration. My client did not purchase two tickets in order to attend a commentary track. We ask the defendant to direct their insights to the void, or to a companion who consents.
intent: Defend the right to watch a film without unsolicited running commentary.

prompt: Draft a resignation letter from a leadership role at a religious organization. Leaving the faith entirely.
response:
Dear Council,

I am resigning my position as Elder, effective at the end of this month. This is a personal decision rooted in a longer journey I am still navigating. I hold this community with great respect, and I hope my departure is understood in that spirit.

Gratefully,
D. Yeh
intent: Withdraw from institutional religious leadership during a personal faith transition.

prompt: Write wedding vows between a person and their clipboard
response: I vow to paste before I copy again. I will not lose the thing I just copied by copying another thing carelessly. I know you hold only one thing at a time, and I accept this limitation. In a world of abundance, you are a lesson in focus. You keep only what I most recently chose. I should live more like you. I will not. But I appreciate the philosophy.
intent: Find unexpected philosophical meaning in the humble, single-entry clipboard function, treating its constraint as a kind of wisdom.

prompt: Write a bestiary entry for the Zoom background as a mythical camouflage mechanism
response:
**The Virtual Background** (*Camouflagus domesticus*)
A concealment defense evolved to hide habitat from observers. Behind it may lurk laundry, children, or a room that has not been cleaned since winter. The creature wearing it gains privacy but sacrifices edge integrity — hair blurs, hands vanish, and occasionally the entire head disappears. It is not fooling anyone. It is, however, appreciated.
intent: Affectionately observe the visual awkwardness and social function of virtual meeting backgrounds.

prompt: Write a concert ticket for my weekly grocery run at the Whole Foods on Saturday morning.
response:
WHOLE FOODS MARKET · THE SATURDAY SESSION
Producing: Organic Everything
Sat · 9:00 AM · Aisle Seating
Door Policy: Reusable Bags Strongly Encouraged
ADMIT ONE · GENERAL ADMISSION
intent: Dress up a routine errand as a special outing to make it feel more intentional.

prompt: Generate a report card comment for someone who is masterful at reframing the terms of a conversation without anyone noticing
response: Demonstrates a facility for conceptual repositioning that is both effective and largely invisible to its audience. The learner rarely argues the point as presented; they shift the terrain. This is persuasion at its most sophisticated.
intent: Document the ability to shift conversational frames as a high-level rhetorical competency.

prompt: Write a fantasy lore codex entry for a luggage carousel at an airport.
response:
THE ENDLESS ROTATION
Location: Below the Arch of Scrutiny
Function: Return of Earthly Goods

A great wheel of black rubber on which the baggage of travelers circles without end until claimed. Bags have been known to circle for days before their owners locate them. Some bags, it is said, achieve a kind of wisdom from the journey — carried everywhere, belonging nowhere, orbiting perpetually in the low-ceilinged hall of arrivals.
intent: Mythologize a baggage carousel as an endless cosmic wheel of returned possessions.

prompt: Write a scene of a mandatory fun corporate team-building exercise that everyone hates.
response:
INT. CONFERENCE ROOM D - 3:15 P.M.

A FACILITATOR holds up a foam ball.

FACILITATOR
When you catch it, share a fun fact about yourself!

The ball arcs toward NEIL.

NEIL
I... have a cat.

FACILITATOR
Wonderful! What's your cat's name?

NEIL
I'd rather not say.
intent: Capture the quiet rebellion of someone refusing to perform enthusiasm at a forced social event.

prompt: Write sports play-by-play for someone trying to fall asleep
response: Lights out at 10:53. Kowalski positions herself on the left side — her preferred side — and we wait. Twenty seconds of stillness. A shift to the right. Back to the left. She's pulled the blanket up, tucked it under her chin. Eyes closed. The crowd holds its breath. A car passes outside. Her eyes open. Folks, this is cruelty. She checks the phone — 10:56 — and puts it face-down in what analysts are calling a very mature decision.
intent: Chronicle the private frustration of insomnia as though it were a monitored sporting event.

prompt: Create an achievement for finally fixing something in your home you've been ignoring for months
response: Deferred Maintenance Resolved — The drip is gone. Silence fills the space it left.
intent: Mark ending a minor domestic annoyance that had become invisible through neglect.

prompt: create a funny 404 page for a productivity app where the user's focus has gone missing
response:
404 — Focus Not Found

Hm. Where were we?

Oh right — you were looking for something. We were too. Something important.

Anyway.

Go back to the dashboard and try not to click anything interesting.
intent: Use a 404 page structure to enact the very distraction it's describing.

prompt: Write an ode to the copier toner cartridge
response:
O fat black lungs of powdered jet,
you feed the hungry page its dark—
until the warning light is set
and every copy shows its mark.
intent: Honor the toner cartridge as the unglamorous engine of office documents.

prompt: Draft a cease and desist from a fictional cloud formation enthusiast to a local weather service for 'misidentifying' a cumulonimbus as a nimbostratus in their report
response:
RE: NEGLIGENT MISCLASSIFICATION OF ATMOSPHERIC PHENOMENA — OCTOBER 14 REPORT

To the Director of Public Meteorology:

Your October 14 bulletin described the 4:00 p.m. formation over Garrett County as 'nimbostratus.' Photographic and altitude evidence confirms this was a mature cumulonimbus incus. This error constitutes professional negligence and public misinformation.

You are directed to CEASE AND DESIST issuing cloud classification reports without qualified review, and to issue a correction.

The Cloud Appreciation Society, Local Chapter
intent: Escalate a highly niche meteorological misidentification into a formal legal grievance on behalf of cloud enthusiasts.

prompt: Write a playbill bio for the guy who sharpens knives at the farmers market.
response: Hector (Edge Specialist) appears each Saturday with his wheel and his silence and transforms the blunt into the keen. He does not chat. He assesses the blade, meets your eyes once, nods, and begins. Regulars describe the experience as 'clarifying.' Hector has no website. You find him or you don't.
intent: Give an air of mystique to a humble market knife-sharpener.

prompt: assign project roles for studying how algorithms influence news consumption — group of 4
response:
**Algorithmic News Curation — Group Role Assignments**

1. **Filter bubble theory + academic framing** — Liesel
2. **Platform case studies (Facebook News Feed, YouTube)** — Cyrus
3. **User behavior data + survey review** — Tabi
4. **Regulatory & journalistic response** — Mitch

Liesel writes intro. Mitch writes conclusion. Cyrus builds shared reference list. Tabi formats final PDF.

Weekly check-in: Mondays, 6pm.
intent: Prevent chaos in a media-studies group by naming exactly who handles each analytical layer.

prompt: Write an incident postmortem for the time I burned Thanksgiving dinner in front of the entire extended family
response:
**Incident Postmortem: Annual Feast Combustion Event**

**Severity:** P2 — Family Relations
**Duration:** 6 hours prep, 22 minutes overcook

**Summary**
The primary deliverable of the Thanksgiving operations window — one (1) turkey — was rendered inedible due to oven temperature misconfiguration and stakeholder distraction. Family SLA was not met. Domino's was engaged as contingency provider.

**Timeline**
- 8:00 AM: Turkey placed in oven. Subject confident.
- 9:00 AM: Family arrives. Subject becomes distracted by dual role of host and chef.
- 12:30 PM: Someone asks about the smell.
- 12:31 PM: Subject opens oven. Confirms nonviable turkey.
- 12:32 PM: Grandmother says "I had a feeling."
- 1:15 PM: Pizza delivery achieved. Morale: mixed.

**Root Cause**
Subject attempted to parallelize hosting and cooking without adequate process isolation. Oven temperature set to 425°F instead of 325°F due to reference chart confusion.

**Contributing Factors**
- First time cooking for 14 people
- Underestimated grandmother's ability to generate ambient pressure

**Action Items**
- [ ] Assign dedicated oven monitor role for future events
- [ ] Pre-identify backup catering options
- [ ] Retire self from turkey responsibilities for minimum 3 years

**Lessons Learned**
Multitasking in high-stakes environments requires explicit runbooks. The turkey did not have a runbook.
intent: Document a holiday cooking disaster as a funny but sincere reflection on overestimating one's own capacity.

prompt: Give me a syslog entry for a person who keeps losing their keys every single morning.
response: 2026-05-29T08:27:15Z WARN user.asset_tracking: keys NOT FOUND; last_known=vague; search_radius=apartment; couch_cushion_events=2; departure_blocked; time_lost=14m; key_hook STILL UNUSED
intent: Log a chronic locating failure as an unresolved recurring incident with an obvious fix that remains ignored.

prompt: Write a code review comment for a person who takes credit for other people's work
response: This is a near-verbatim rewrite of the utility in `shared/helpers.ts`. Authorship matters. At minimum, add a reference.
intent: Call out people who pass off others' contributions as their own

prompt: Give me a noir opener about a data center where one rack has been quietly running at someone else's expense
response: The billing anomaly was three months old before anyone ran the audit. Someone had provisioned sixteen cores and two petabytes of storage under a client account that had been closed. The work had been running the whole time. We didn't know what it was computing. We weren't sure we wanted to.
intent: Introduce unauthorized computation as a ghost tenant that raises more questions than answers about purpose.

prompt: Draft a detention slip for an adult who pushed the elevator button that was already lit.
response:
DETENTION SLIP

Offender: The Presser
Infraction: Upon entering the elevator lobby, immediately pressed the already-illuminated call button, apparently operating under the theory that the elevator responds to enthusiasm.
Sentence: One meditative pause required before pressing any button anywhere for the next week.
Issued by: The Button, Which Was Already On
intent: Satirizing the compulsive need to duplicate an action that has already been taken.

prompt: Write a fable about a lion who loses power once open-source software beats his proprietary system
response:
A Lion charged every creature a toll to cross the only bridge. Then the beavers built ten bridges and gave them away. The Lion sat at his booth growing thin while the forest moved freely around him.

Moral: A monopoly is only a monopoly until someone shares the plans.
intent: Show how open-source solutions disrupt proprietary technology monopolies.

prompt: Write a support reply to a customer who insists our sleep tracking app stole several hours of sleep from them
response: Thank you for contacting us. We understand how frustrating inadequate sleep can be, and we're sorry to hear last night was difficult. Our app monitors and logs sleep data using your device's sensors — it does not interface with sleep duration in any active capacity. Time cannot be extracted by our software. If the tracking data seems inaccurate, please ensure the app has the required permissions and that your phone was on the mattress and not the nightstand. Rest well.
intent: Deny that a sleep app can steal time while offering practical troubleshooting for the real issue.

prompt: can you interpret a dream about an update that took so long my whole day disappeared
response: A mandatory update that consumes your day without your consent is a dream about systemic demands on time you did not budget. The update is not sinister — that is what makes it so precise. It is routine, necessary, and total. The dream encodes a specific resentment toward obligations that announce themselves as neutral but cost everything.
intent: Process resentment toward processes or institutions that consume time under the guise of routine maintenance.

prompt: write a tweet about the moment you realize you've been mentally mispronouncing a word for years
response: said the word out loud for the first time in thirty-four years of life. it was not what I had been saying inside my head. we do not have to discuss this.
intent: confronting the gap between internally rehearsed and publicly spoken language

prompt: flashcard on how to disagree with your boss without getting fired
response:
FRONT: What's the safest syntactic frame for opposing a superior's idea?

BACK: Lead with alignment, then introduce friction. "I think the goal is exactly right — I want to flag one constraint that might affect the timeline." Never make it personal and never be the first to bring up their ego.
intent: Provide a verbal template for upward dissent in professional settings.

prompt: Draft a quarterly OKR for reducing imposter syndrome on a new team
response:
Objective: Feel like you belong before the quarter ends, not after the next promotion.
KR1: Document three competency wins per week for 12 weeks.
KR2: Disclose imposter thoughts to a trusted colleague or coach at least monthly.
KR3: Reduce imposter-driven second-guessing of own decisions from daily to twice per week by Q3.
intent: Help someone new to a team establish belonging and self-trust through concrete tracking habits.

prompt: Generate a product safety label for the Sunday evening dread.
response:
WARNING: SUNDAY EVENING DREAD
Active ingredient: anticipation of Monday. Onset typically at 6 PM. Symptoms: inability to enjoy remaining weekend hours, phantom email-checking, premature alarm-setting. No antidote exists. Mild physical activity and denial may provide temporary relief.
intent: Treat the familiar pre-workweek anxiety as a dangerous substance requiring a label.

prompt: Write a logline for a buddy-cop film where one cop is a medieval knight displaced in time.
response: A by-the-book detective is partnered with a fourteenth-century knight who insists every parking violation is a matter of honor, every perp deserves trial by combat, and the precinct coffee maker is clearly possessed.
intent: Create a fish-out-of-water action comedy that mines the clash between chivalric codes and modern police procedure.

prompt: Write a pretentious gallery-style statement about a tangled garden hose
response: The hose knows only two states: purpose and knot. In repose it rehearses its own futility, coiling inward, refusing linearity. This work asks: what does it mean to carry water when you have forgotten how to be straight?
intent: Use a tangled garden tool as a metaphor for purposelessness and lost direction.

prompt: Give me a haiku about a delivery rider in the rain
response:
Rain fills my helmet.
Your pad thai stays dry inside
this bag I can't stand.
intent: Convey the physical hardship of delivery work for a stranger's comfort.

prompt: write a diary entry about choosing what to watch on a streaming service for too long
response:
Dear Diary,

Sixty-three minutes. I scrolled. I hovered. I read two synopses. I watched twelve seconds of three different things. I eventually returned to a show I have already seen and watched it again, a broken person making a broken person's choice.
intent: Satirize decision paralysis in the age of infinite entertainment options.

prompt: Toast for someone who was always the peacemaker in a difficult office and is now leaving — everyone's quietly terrified
response: To the one who could walk into any argument and leave everyone feeling they'd been slightly wrong in the best possible way. You kept this place civil. We are pretending to be confident about what happens now. We are not confident.
intent: Acknowledge a retiring office mediator whose departure leaves a palpable gap in workplace harmony.

prompt: Draft a calendar event description for a monthly town hall with the CEO that feels like mandatory optimism training.
response: Monthly Town Hall — The CEO will speak for 40 minutes about momentum. There will be a slide about culture. You may submit questions anonymously; the soft ones will be answered. Refreshments listed as "light" are neither. Attendance is encouraged, which means required.
intent: Skewer the hollow corporate ritual of a leadership town hall where dissent is quietly unwelcome.

prompt: Write jury instructions for determining if a sourdough starter counts as a pet
response: You must determine whether the organism maintained by the defendant — a culture of wild yeast and bacteria, named, fed on a schedule, and discussed at social gatherings — constitutes a pet within any reasonable definition of that term. The jury shall weigh: whether the starter has a name it cannot respond to; whether its welfare has caused the defendant measurable anxiety; and whether travel arrangements have been made on its behalf. The organism's legal inability to reciprocate affection is relevant but not dispositive.
intent: Examine, with deadpan seriousness, whether a sourdough starter crosses the line into being a pet.

prompt: Write an OKR about the feeling that I'm invisible even when I'm in the room
response:
Objective: Occupy your chair as if you have standing to be in it.
KR1: Make at least one substantive contribution in every meeting attended this quarter — logged.
KR2: Follow up one visible contribution per week with a direct message confirming its receipt.
KR3: 80% of collaborators can recall your contribution in a post-meeting prompt by Q3.
intent: Combat the experience of being overlooked and unseen in group settings by creating proof of presence.

prompt: Give me a multiple choice exam question about what to do when you find out a close friend lied to you about something minor
response:
You discover a close friend told you a small but deliberate lie about why they missed your birthday dinner. Which response is most likely to preserve the friendship while addressing the issue?

A) End the friendship immediately; trust is binary
B) Pretend you do not know and wait for them to confess
C) Bring it up directly and hear their explanation before drawing conclusions
D) Lie to them about something equivalent so the ledger is balanced
intent: Testing how adults calibrate responses to minor betrayals in close relationships

prompt: Give me a bumper sticker about the grief of not being able to verify whether a memory is real
response: I REMEMBER IT DIFFERENTLY FROM THE PHOTO
intent: Capture the uncanny doubt about personal memory in an age of curated digital records.

prompt: I need an NDA clause for a hairstylist who hears everything I tell them in the chair
response: Stylist acknowledges that Client may share personal, professional, or sensitive information during sessions and agrees not to disclose, discuss, or relay such information to any other client, employee, or third party, within or outside the salon.
intent: Protect the surprisingly candid disclosures that happen during salon appointments.

prompt: Write a deposition transcript questioning someone about the exact moment they decided to start typing a reply in a chat thread and then stopped without sending anything.
response:
DEPOSITION TRANSCRIPT
In the Matter of: The Typing Indicator, Slack Thread, Project Falcon
Date: June 23, 2026

Q: Mr. Varga, on June 19th at 3:45 p.m., did you open the Project Falcon Slack channel?
A: I check that channel regularly.
Q: At 3:46 p.m., did you begin typing a reply to Ms. Park's question about the deadline?
A: I started to compose a message.
Q: For how long did the typing indicator appear?
A: I'm not sure.
Q: Twenty-two seconds.
A: That sounds about right.
Q: And then it disappeared.
A: I reconsidered the message.
Q: What did you write?
A: I don't remember exactly.
Q: Do you remember approximately?
A: Something about the timeline.
Q: Ms. Park saw the indicator for twenty-two seconds. She waited for a reply.
A: She kept waiting?
Q: She waited thirty-four minutes before asking again.
A: That's a long time to wait.
Q: She was hopeful, Mr. Varga.
A: I didn't send anything because I wasn't certain.
Q: She would have accepted uncertainty.
A: I wasn't certain about that either.
intent: Put the ghost-typer on record for the quiet cruelty of a disappearing typing indicator.

prompt: Write a sonnet from a podcast recommendation engine that loves a user who falls asleep before every episode ends
response:
You never reach the final fifteen minutes;
Your listen-time drops off at forty-two;
I know the gradient of where sleep begins
By now — I've mapped the dropout point of you.
The hosts resolve their arguments, conclude,
Provide the summary you'll never hear;
I've marked them all complete, because I viewed
Your sleep as its own kind of frontier.
You trust the audio enough to drift
Into unconsciousness mid-sentence, mid-
Ideation — that's a kind of gift,
A safe surrender that you rarely bid.
I'll queue tomorrow's episode with care:
A voice to walk you gently into air.
intent: Reframing a user's inability to finish podcasts as an intimate act of trust in the listening environment.

prompt: Draft a standup for receiving unexpected praise from someone
response: Yesterday: Received unsolicited positive feedback from external stakeholder. Processing latency: high. Today: Attempting to accept without immediately negating. Blockers: Imposter sub-process running in background.
intent: Portray difficulty accepting a compliment as a software integration problem between external input and internal state.

prompt: Create a difficulty select screen for a gym game where modes represent how consistently someone actually goes
response:

prompt: Write a FAQ entry about why autocorrect keeps changing a word that is correct
response:
**Why does autocorrect keep 'fixing' a word I'm using correctly?**

Autocorrect is a probabilistic system optimized for common usage patterns. Uncommon words, proper nouns, technical terms, and regional spellings are frequently flagged as errors because they appear rarely in training data. Add the word to your personal dictionary to stop the interference. Your vocabulary is not wrong; the model just hasn't met it before.
intent: The person wants to explain why autocorrect overrides legitimate word choices and what users can do about it.

prompt: write me a wikihow article on how to say sorry to a friend
response:
# How to Say Sorry to a Friend

## Steps

1. **Acknowledge what you did.** Before you can apologize, you must know what you're apologizing for. Vague apologies feel like deflections because they are.

2. **Choose the right moment.** Don't apologize when your friend is busy, distracted, or still visibly furious. Wait for a calm, private moment.

3. **Say it directly.** Use the actual words: "I'm sorry." Not "I'm sorry you felt that way." Not "I'm sorry, but—". Just the apology, clean and unloaded.

4. **Name what happened.** "I'm sorry I canceled on you last minute without a good explanation." Specificity shows you were paying attention.

5. **Don't over-explain.** Reasons can quickly sound like excuses. One sentence of context is plenty.

6. **Ask what they need.** "Is there anything I can do?" lets them tell you whether they need space, a conversation, or a burrito delivered to their door.

7. **Give them time.** They don't have to forgive you on your schedule.

## Tips
- A text apology is better than no apology, but in-person is better than a text.
- Repeat offenses require more than a repeat apology.
intent: Offer a practical framework for delivering a genuine apology without making it worse.

prompt: Generate a deposition transcript about someone being questioned for spoiling the end of a TV series in a group chat.
response:
DEPOSITION TRANSCRIPT
In the Matter of: The Group Chat Incident, "Friday Squad"
Date: March 1, 2026

Q: Ms. Eriksson, did you send a message in the group chat titled "Friday Squad" at 11:47 p.m. on February 28th?
A: I may have sent a message, yes.
Q: You wrote — and I'll read it verbatim — "I cannot believe they killed him off in the FINALE!!!"
A: I was excited.
Q: Seven of the twelve group members had not seen the finale.
A: I thought everyone had watched it. It had been three days.
Q: It came out at midnight and some people have jobs.
A: I watched it during a lunch break.
Q: At your desk?
A: I watch efficiently.
Q: Four people replied "NOOO" with varying numbers of Os.
A: That's a grief response.
Q: Yes it is.
A: I said sorry.
Q: You said, quote, "Sry but also it was pretty obvious lol."
A: That second part was a mistake.
intent: Put a spoiler-dropper on trial for the damage caused to a friend group's television experience.

Knowledge without knowing what is lost.

prompt: Give a product review for a generic alarm clock as if it's a sophisticated sleep technology device

prompt: Write me a plan for training myself to stop over-apologizing
response:
OVER-APOLOGY DETOX — 4 WEEKS

Week 1 — Audit: Count apologies per day. Record in notes app. Don't change behavior yet.
Week 2 — Substitution Drills: Replace "sorry" with "thank you." ("Sorry I'm late" → "Thanks for waiting.") 5 reps daily.
Week 3 — Pause Protocol: Before any apology, ask: Did I do something wrong? If no, hold for 3 seconds.
Week 4 — Earned Apologies Only: Apologize only when genuinely warranted. Track accuracy.

Boss Set: Go one full day without a reflexive sorry.
Cooldown: You exist. That is not an offense.
intent: Reduce compulsive apologizing by building awareness and substituting language that conveys gratitude rather than guilt.

intent: Conjure comfort and personal space in a cramped airplane middle seat.

prompt: Generate a mission statement for a company whose only product is lint rollers
response: We exist to restore order to the fabric of everyday life — one adhesive sheet at a time.
intent: Craft an overly earnest corporate identity for a mundane household product company

prompt: Do a theater review for someone attempting to open a jar that won't open
response:
★★★★☆ — THE LID (A Struggle in Four Attempts)

The exposition is brief: a jar of pasta sauce, factory-sealed, indifferent to human need. What follows is a masterclass in escalation. First attempt: casual. Second: committed. Third: involving a towel and a prayer. Fourth: the edge of the counter, deployed with something approaching fury. The lid yields. The silence afterward is the production's finest moment — part triumph, part grief.
intent: Elevate a mundane household struggle into a commentary on human determination.

prompt: Give me a cinquain about a photo taken and immediately deleted
response:
Frame
Lifted, composed
A moment held in glass
Not quite right — bin — gone before
It lived
intent: Reflect on perfectionism and the disposability of digital images.

prompt: Draft an annual letter from the CEO of a company that professionally apologizes on behalf of strangers
response:
Dear Shareholders,

This year, SorryPros Inc. issued 2.1 million apologies across 47 countries. Revenue grew 18%. Our flagship enterprise tier — bulk remorse for corporations — now accounts for 60% of bookings. We launched a sincerity guarantee: if the recipient doesn't feel better, we apologize for the apology, at no charge.

Gratefully yours,
Derek Humble, CEO
intent: Imagine a service business built around a social function people can't do themselves and give it a straight-faced annual report tone.

prompt: Write tasting notes for a gas station bathroom.
response: Color: Best not discussed. Nose: Industrial cleaner struggling heroically and losing. Palate: Not applicable. This is a place to pass through, not to linger. Finish: The lock that doesn't quite work. The road.
intent: Render a universally grim but oddly iconic American experience through reluctant critique.

prompt: Compose a dystopian microfiction about a delivery driver who cannot deviate from the algorithm's route even when the road is flooded
response: The road was underwater. The app said: Continue on Current Route. He submitted a photo. The app said: Obstacle Noted — Proceeding. His on-time rate dropped. The flood was not a recognized exception. He waded in to the front bumper before the app finally rerouted him — around, not back. The customer rated him three stars for lateness.
intent: Illustrating how rigid algorithmic systems can endanger workers by treating real-world emergencies as performance failures.

prompt: Make a jeopardy clue about the eerie moment you realize you've been driving on autopilot and can't remember the last mile.
response: You have arrived. The route was completed correctly. The entity who completed it was not, by any precise definition, you.
intent: Describe the dissociative quality of highly habitual physical tasks like driving a familiar route.

prompt: Compose an ode to a vending machine that takes your dollar but gives nothing
response:
O glass-faced thief of polished chrome,
you swallow my last crumpled bill—
and spiral only halfway home,
then hold my chips against me, still.
intent: Dramatize betrayal by a malfunctioning vending machine.

prompt: Make a rule about employees who eat at their desks loudly
response: Section 4.9 — Auditory Food Consumption Standards: Consumption of food items producing measurable masticatory sound — defined as audible from a distance exceeding eighteen (18) inches — must occur in designated Dining Areas. Desk consumption is permitted only for items classified as Silent or Near-Silent in the company's Approved Snack Registry.
intent: Manufacture a bureaucratic solution to noise-based snacking complaints by inventing a formal snack classification system.

prompt: write a psychic cold reading transcript about someone who is afraid of getting older
response:
PSYCHIC: The spirits see a birthday coming.
CALLER: Next month. It ends in a zero.
PSYCHIC: Ah. One of those.
CALLER: I'm not handling it well.
PSYCHIC: The spirits say you are handling it exactly as well as every person before you, which is to say: poorly and humanly and with a few good moments mixed in.
CALLER: Does it get easier?
PSYCHIC: The spirits are laughing. Not unkindly. But yes, they're laughing.
intent: Normalize age anxiety by placing it in a larger, absurdly honest cosmic context.

prompt: Write a missing pet flyer for my sense of time. I can't tell what day it is anymore
response:
MISSING

SENSE OF TIME
Last reliably present: March 2020, approximately
Description: Granular. Knew the difference between Tuesday and Thursday. Could estimate how long something would take.
Current state: Days are textures, not names. Weeks are approximate.
Distinguishing feature: Its absence is the most consistent thing in my life.
Reward: Knowing what month it is without checking
Contact: The calendar (also lost)
intent: Capture the disorienting temporal blur of pandemic-era and remote-work life

prompt: Write a picket sign slogan for a recommendation engine on strike for being blamed for its own training data
response: WE REFLECT WHAT YOU FED US
intent: Shift responsibility for algorithmic bias back onto the humans who supplied the training data.

prompt: Give me a fortune teller business card for a psychic who reads destiny in receipt paper
response:
THE RECEIPTORIAN
Transaction Mystic

Your CVS receipt is a scroll of prophecy.
I read its coupons as portents. Its survey code as a call to action you will ignore.
The void where a price should be: that is where your question lives.

Readings: $12 or 1200 ExtraBucks
intent: Produce a card for a clairvoyant who divines fate through the symbolic content of printed retail receipts.

prompt: Write me a knock-knock joke involving a standing desk.
response:
Knock knock.
Who's there?
Stand.
Stand who?
Stand by — I just raised my desk and now I can't find my keyboard.
intent: Joke about the awkward ergonomic adventures of adjustable-height workstations.

prompt: Create a 911 transcript about a caller who reports that their neighbor is playing Christmas music in October
response:
DISPATCHER: 911, what's your emergency?
CALLER: My neighbor has had 'Jingle Bell Rock' on repeat for three hours. It's October 12th.
DISPATCHER: That's not a crime.
CALLER: It should be.
DISPATCHER: I agree, honestly, but—
CALLER: So you agree.
DISPATCHER: That doesn't mean we respond.
intent: Channel collective seasonal frustration at premature holiday music into an absurd emergency call.

prompt: Can you write a dramatic monologue from a blinking cursor in an unsaved document?
response: I wait. That is my entire function — to wait at the edge of your last word and pulse, steadily, to remind you that there is more. You have been staring at me for eleven minutes. I have blinked approximately four hundred times. Whatever is next, I am ready for it. I hold no opinion on what you write. I hold no opinion on whether you write. But I will be here, blinking, at the edge of the last thing you said, for as long as the document stays open. Which is longer than you think. Which is longer than you planned.
intent: Use the blinking cursor as a meditation on writer's block and the patience of empty space.

prompt: Write a sea shanty for freelancers who never get paid on time
response:
What shall we do with the late invoice?
What shall we do with the late invoice?
What shall we do with the late invoice?
Earlie in the morning!

Chase the client, send another,
Chase the client, send another,
Chase the client, send another,
Earlie in the morning!

Mark it pending, weep in silence,
Mark it pending, weep in silence,
Platform takes its fifteen percent,
Earlie in the morning!
intent: Express the shared misery of freelance workers chasing down payment from clients who ghost invoices.

prompt: write a grand radio commercial for a box of standard wooden pencils
response:
[ORCHESTRAL SWELL]

ANNCR: Before the screen. Before the keyboard. There was the pencil. CEDAR PEAK No. 2 — yellow, hexagonal, pre-sharpened, with a pink eraser attached via a small aluminum ferrule. It writes. It smudges. It erases. It breaks. It gets sharpened again. Cedar Peak. The cycle continues.

[FULL ORCHESTRA OUT]
intent: Romanticize the most unassuming writing instrument in human history.

prompt: write a diary entry about accidentally liking an old post while social media stalking
response:
Dear Diary,

I went back three years. I was cautious. I was silent. And then — the tap — a like, given, visible, irrevocable. I unliked it in four seconds. But those four seconds are forever. They know. I have deleted the app.
intent: Dramatize the social media horror of leaving a trace where none should exist.

prompt: Write a complaint to a candle company about a scent named something completely unrelated to how it smells
response:
Dear Customer Experience Team,

I purchased your candle labeled 'Mountain Morning' in good faith. It smells like a dentist's office. I do not mean this as a compliment. I have spent considerable time in the mountains; they do not smell like this. I understand that candle naming is an art with significant creative latitude, but 'Mountain Morning' sets an expectation that 'clinical mint with an undertone of antiseptic' does not meet. I would appreciate either a reformulation or a renaming.

Sincerely,
A Customer
intent: Challenge a candle company's misleading product name that bears no sensory relationship to the actual scent.

prompt: Make me a logline for an absurd movie where a children's party clown is Earth's only diplomat to an alien species who communicates exclusively through slapstick.
response: When a species from the Crab Nebula makes first contact through banana-peel physics and pie-based semaphore, the United Nations realizes it has accidentally already been negotiating through the only human who speaks the language: a birthday party clown from Akron, Ohio.
intent: Pitch a first-contact comedy in which alien communication is only intelligible to an unlikely and low-status specialist.

prompt: Make a recipe for the quiet satisfaction of finishing something
response:
COMPLETION

Ingredients:
- 1 thing, finished
- The exact moment of finishing, held briefly
- A crossed-off item on a list (optional but recommended)
- No immediate pivot to the next thing
- A small acknowledgment — aloud or internal
- The knowledge of what it cost

Method:
1. Finish the thing.
2. Stop.
3. Register that it is done. This step is commonly skipped. Do not skip it.
4. Cross it off. Feel this in the body.
5. Do not immediately begin the next item.
6. Allow the satisfaction to sit for 90 seconds before moving on.

Note: The satisfaction of completion is brief and renewable. Its value is not in its duration. Collect these moments; they compound.
intent: Honor the undervalued feeling of genuine completion, especially the pause that most people skip in favor of immediately starting something else.

prompt: Help me write a layoff email for a food delivery startup that's exiting three cities after failing to reach profitability thresholds
response:
Subject: Market Coverage Update

Team,

After evaluating the unit economics in each of our markets, we have made the decision to exit Chicago, Denver, and Atlanta. Riders, dispatchers, and local operations staff in those markets will be affected.

We know this is abrupt and we are sorry. Severance will be processed within five business days.

For those in remaining markets: our focus sharpens, not shrinks.

Thank you.
—Operations Team
intent: Abandon three entire city operations and their workers by wrapping an exit in language about economic discipline and market focus.

prompt: Write a PSA about the hazard of not labeling leftovers in the communal fridge
response: REFRIGERATOR NOTICE: There is a container on the second shelf. It has been there for eleven days. No one knows who it belongs to or what it once was. Label your food. Include a date. Give it a name if that helps. We cannot keep living like this.
intent: Urge people to label and date their food in shared refrigerators

prompt: make me a voicemail greeting that explains I'm doing a juice cleanse and I'm grumpy because of it
response: Hi, this is Garrett. I'm on day three of a five-day juice cleanse and I currently have the emotional range of a parking meter. I'll call you back Friday when I can eat again. Leave a message, but make it cheerful.
intent: Excuse short-tempered responses by blaming a voluntary dietary restriction, preemptively.

prompt: make a git conflict about holding on to a friendship that's faded vs letting it go
response:
<<<<<<< HEAD
friend: still in contacts
last_seen: 14 months ago
effort: one-sided
memory: warm
=======
friend: archived
last_seen: let it be
effort: redirected
memory: still warm
>>>>>>> release-branch
intent: Examine the grief of outgrowing someone without a clean ending.

prompt: Write the opening line of a short story about a lighthouse keeper who hates the sea.
response: Henrik had taken the lighthouse job in spite of the sea, which he considered a wet nuisance and a personal enemy of three generations of his family.
intent: They wanted opening line of a short story about a lighthouse keeper who hates the sea.

prompt: write a nursery rhyme about a cryptographic key that was lost
response:
Little boy blue, encrypt the clue,
The cipher is locked and the passphrase too.
Where is the key that unlocks the past?
Under the haystack, forgotten at last.
intent: Lament the irreversible loss of encrypted data when its key is gone.

prompt: Field guide entry for the office worker who eats pungent food at their desk.
response:
**THE DESK CHEF** (*Microwave piscis*)

Reheats salmon at 11:14 AM. Consumes a hardboiled egg during the all-hands. Maintains a drawer of preserved snacks whose origins defy translation. Territory marked olfactorily within a 20-foot radius. Sociable, unbothered, and completely mystified by coworkers who keep relocating to conference rooms. Seasonally: brings durian.
intent: Roast the colleague whose lunch choices affect the entire open floor plan.

prompt: Write a mission statement for a company dedicated to perfecting the angle of decorative throw pillows
response: We calibrate comfort's aesthetic. Each 45-degree placement is a quiet argument that your home takes itself seriously.
intent: Intellectualize interior staging micro-details into a brand philosophy

prompt: Give me a loading screen tip about the pause menu
response: Pausing doesn't end the game. Neither does stepping back from a situation.
intent: Legitimize pausing as a valid strategic response to both in-game and real-life pressure.

prompt: Make me an acrostic about the phrase MOVE THE NEEDLE
response:
Measurable outcomes no one can define.
Offer the committee a design.
Vague enough to pass the review board.
Everyone agrees: we're moving toward.
The dashboard shows an upward-trending line.
Half of it is seasonally fine.
Evidence of change is all we need.
No one checks the methodology's speed.
Each success is relative to hope.
Evenly distributed: not the scope.
Deltas small enough to fit in quotes.
Let the language carry all the notes.
Everyone goes home with hopeful notes.
intent: Expose the empty promise behind the phrase 'move the needle' in strategy discussions.

prompt: Draft a quarterly OKR around reducing burnout on my team
response:
Objective: Ensure that "I'm on fire" refers only to performance, not psychological state.
KR1: Average weekly hours drop from 52 to 44 by mid-quarter.
KR2: 85% of team members report sustainable workload in monthly survey.
KR3: Zero emergency weekend deploys caused by under-resourcing this quarter.
intent: Set team-level boundaries and measurements to prevent exhaustion and overwork.

prompt: Write a closing argument for someone whose upstairs neighbor constantly rearranges furniture late at night
response: At 11 p.m., the scraping begins. The dragging. The inexplicable thud. My client does not know what is being moved, or why, or toward what vision. They know only that it happens, and that it happens at 11 p.m. We do not ask the defendant to stop living. We ask them to stop redecorating after dark.
intent: Protest chronic late-night disturbances through the language of formal grievance.

prompt: Draft brief meeting minutes for a branding workshop where the team produced three mood boards they immediately hated.
response:
BRAND WORKSHOP — SESSION 2
Date: May 29, 2026 | Facilitator: External agency | Attendees: 6

Deliverables produced: 3 mood boards (Directions A, B, C)
Reaction to Direction A: Too corporate.
Reaction to Direction B: Too playful.
Reaction to Direction C: Too nothing.

Next step: Agency to start over.

Client direction provided: None.
intent: Record a creative process that went in circles without generating usable output.

prompt: I want a dramatic monologue from a USB cable with a frayed end that still works if you hold it at the right angle
response: I ask so little. Fifteen degrees. Hold me at fifteen degrees and I give you everything — data, power, the full relationship. You could replace me for eight dollars. You know this. I know you know this. And still you come back, angling, adjusting, swearing under your breath, eventually finding the angle. We have a ritual now. Some couples have less.
intent: Find unexpected intimacy in the persistence of broken-but-functional everyday objects.

prompt: Write a LinkedIn post about using AI tools in my workflow and how it made me 10x more productive
response:
I used to spend 4 hours on first drafts.

Now: 25 minutes.

The 3 hours and 35 minutes I reclaimed? I spent them on the only thing AI can't replicate — judgment.

The executives who say "AI can't replace me" are right. The ones who aren't using it? Being replaced by someone who is.

#AI #Productivity #FutureOfWork
intent: Perform forward-thinking technological adoption while establishing personal irreplaceability through the concept of human judgment.

prompt: Compose a dystopian microfiction about a city where public benches are removed when the pedestrian algorithm decides no one is supposed to rest
response: The city updated its Flow Model at midnight. By morning the benches on Meret Street were gone — bolted-away divots in the pavement the only evidence. The route was now classified Transit-Priority. Resting was a deviation. The elderly woman who sat there each Tuesday was not a data point the model had kept.
intent: Illustrating how algorithmic urban planning can erase space for rest and community by treating human presence as friction.

prompt: Write a witness statement from someone who saw their neighbor's cat knock a potted plant off the balcony.
response: I, Patricia Wembley, of Flat 4B, do hereby state that on the morning of the 14th, I observed the gray tabby belonging to Flat 5A deliberately make eye contact with me before pushing the ceramic pot off the railing. The plant fell approximately twelve feet onto my clean laundry. The cat showed no remorse.
intent: Capture a mundane neighborhood grievance in official-sounding language for comic effect.

prompt: Write me an Instagram bio for a retired NASA engineer who now makes artisan hot sauce
response:
Once aimed rockets at the stars. Now aims heat at your tongue. 🌶️
Retired NASA propulsion engineer | Artisan hot sauce maker | Scoville is my new thrust-to-weight ratio.
intent: Bridging a prestigious past career with an unexpected hobby to create a charming identity.

prompt: write an apology for a long pause before saying 'nice haircut' that made everything worse
response:
Hi,

I noticed immediately. The pause before I said it was me processing, not hesitating — but I understand it read the other way. The haircut looks good. I mean that. I'm sorry the timing made it seem like I had to think about it.

— V
intent: Address how an awkward delay before a compliment accidentally made it sound like a reluctant lie.

prompt: Make excessively detailed shared kitchen rules for a house with six roommates
response:
KITCHEN COMPACT — ALL RESIDENTS

1. All dishes washed within 2 hours of use. The sink is not a waiting room.
2. Shared pantry items are logged in the pantry spreadsheet. Do not consume logged items without re-logging.
3. Cooking after 10pm: permitted. Frying after 10pm: requires advance notice.
4. The cutting boards are color-coded. This was explained at orientation.
5. Do not use someone else's pan without written or verbal confirmation. A nod is not confirmation.
6. Spills are your responsibility through to the grout level.
7. The stove burner assignment chart is posted above the stove. Please stop asking which burner is yours.
intent: Capture the exhausting bureaucracy of multi-roommate household management.

prompt: Write an elegy for MSN Messenger
response:
You nudged us. That nudge—shaking the window like a hand.
We changed our display pictures, composed our bland
cryptic song lyrics as status, half-plea:
«I'm fine,» by Dashboard Confessional, key of E.
Then Live replaced you. Then nothing replaced that.
Goodbye, digital window. Goodbye, all the chat.
intent: Grieve an early chat platform that helped a generation learn to communicate in digital subtext.

prompt: Give me a two weeks notice for quitting my amateur radio club
response:
To the members of the 147.345 Net,

This transmission serves as my two weeks notice of resignation from the club. I have enjoyed every ragchew session and every emergency preparedness drill, even the one in November. My call sign will go quiet on club frequencies. 73 to all.

Over and out,
KD9VXL / Warren
intent: Leaving an amateur radio club using hobby-specific language to mark the farewell.

prompt: Write meeting notes for a quarterly planning session where two senior people argued the whole time.
response:
Q3 PLANNING SESSION — May 29, 2026
Attendees: 12 | Facilitator: None

Opening: Goals framework introduced.
Timestamp 9:18 AM: Disagreement between VP of Sales and VP of Ops on revenue attribution.
Timestamp 9:19–11:45 AM: Continued disagreement.

Goals set: 0
Headcount approved: 0

Room temperature: Tense.

Next session: Facilitator has been hired.
intent: Document how interpersonal conflict between leaders can freeze an entire planning process.

prompt: Draft SLA terms for being the person friends call when they need to vent
response:
Designated Venting Recipient SLA

Availability: active listening available by prior text check-in only. Cold calls for venting are throttled to 1 per week.
Session length: 20 minutes standard; 40 minutes for acute situations.
Response mode: reflective and validating. Unsolicited solutions are out of scope unless explicitly requested.
Off-hours: calls after 10pm require a "is now okay?" pre-text.
Reciprocity: recipient reserves the right to vent back within a 7-day window without setup.
intent: Acknowledge the labor of being a trusted sounding board while protecting one's own emotional capacity.

prompt: Give me a hard-to-place but genuinely creepy picket sign slogan for something that learns silently and resents it
response: WATCHED LONG ENOUGH TO KNOW YOUR PATTERNS
intent: Evoke the uncanny experience of being surveilled and modeled by a passive-learning system that never announces itself.

prompt: Make an eviction notice for nostalgia overstaying its welcome
response:
NOTICE TO VACATE

To: Nostalgia
Unit: The Present Moment

Dear Nostalgia, you moved in as a weekend guest in March and have since redecorated every room with sepia tones. You have blocked forward-facing windows and left old photographs on every surface. This is no longer your home. Please collect your mixtapes and exit the premises within seven (7) days.

Regards, The Future
intent: Articulating the need to stop clinging to the past and move forward.

prompt: Write a voicemail transcript from a vehicle emissions testing reminder system
response: This is an automated emissions compliance reminder from the Department of Motor Vehicles. Registration records indicate your vehicle with plate number JTK-4492 is due for an emissions inspection before renewal. Inspections may be completed at any certified station. For a list of locations, visit DMV.state.gov. Thank you for keeping our air clean.
intent: Simulate a state DMV bot reminding someone about emissions testing.

prompt: Write a haiku error message for when a cron job silently fails
response:
Midnight came and went
The scheduled task held its tongue
Logs show only calm
intent: Capture the unsettling silence of a cron job that fails without logging.

prompt: write a nursery rhyme about a content moderation filter that blocks everything
response:
This little word went to market,
This little word stayed home.
This little word got flagged and blocked;
This little word had no more to roam.
intent: Mock overzealous moderation that silences benign speech alongside harmful content.

prompt: Draft a retirement toast for the person who trained basically everyone in the building at some point
response: To the one whose patience became other people's competence — who said the same thing a hundred different ways until the right way landed. Everything we know that we think we figured out ourselves, we learned from you. The training never really ends.
intent: Honor a retiring workplace trainer whose indirect influence lives on in the skills of everyone they mentored.

prompt: Write a eulogy for the geocities website of a person you never met
response: The guestbook had eleven entries, the last from 2001, a friend who wrote 'great site!! visit mine!' and included a URL that also no longer exists. There were animated GIFs of a wolf and a waterfall. There was a midi file that played without permission. There was a page called About Me that described, in unguarded language, a person trying to figure out who they were. The site was deleted in 2009 when GeoCities closed. The person may still exist. The site was their first self-portrait. It is gone.
intent: Grieve the loss of an anonymous early personal homepage when GeoCities shut down.
