prompt: Write a commit message about going through therapy
response:
refactor: audit and restructure core emotional logic

Technical debt identified in childhood modules. Slow process. Compiling.
intent: Describe therapy as a long-overdue internal code review and architectural cleanup.

prompt: Give me a fortune cookie about automated customer service chatbots
response: Your frustration has been noted, categorized, and escalated to a queue with a four-hour wait.
intent: To mock the customer experience of dealing with automated service systems that defer rather than resolve.

prompt: Create a job ad for a Chief Explainability Officer who makes sure a company can explain its AI decisions to anyone
response:
CHIEF EXPLAINABILITY OFFICER
Executive | Hybrid | $200,000–$250,000 + equity

The regulator asked why. 'The model said so' is not a sufficient answer. You will provide the sufficient answer.

As CXO, you will build the organizational capability to explain, document, and defend every consequential AI-driven decision made by this company: to customers, to regulators, to courts, and to our own board.

This requires technical depth, policy fluency, and the rare ability to hold both simultaneously. You will report to the CEO and be uncomfortable at most of your meetings. That is the nature of the role.

Apply only if you believe the problem is solvable.
intent: Hire a senior executive to lead the company-wide effort of making AI decisions interpretable and defensible.

prompt: Give me a scene where a long-tenured employee refuses to learn new software.
response:
INT. CUBICLE - 3:00 P.M.

IRENE has the training window open. She also has Excel 2003 open. She is using Excel 2003.

IT TECHNICIAN
Irene, the new system—

IRENE
Works for me.

IT TECHNICIAN
It's being deprecated.

IRENE
So am I.
intent: Use resistance to technology as a quiet statement about aging in a workplace built on planned obsolescence.

prompt: Write a condolence card for someone who dropped their habit of practicing piano every morning
response:
The bench sits empty at seven a.m.

Scales, arpeggios, that one passage you never quite got right — all of it was becoming something. Muscle memory was winning, slowly, as it does.

The keys do not go cold. They wait with extraordinary patience.

With love and a soft pedal.
intent: Comfort someone whose daily piano practice routine was quietly abandoned.

prompt: Compose a eulogy for my old AIM screen name
response: XxDarkWolf2003xX, you lived a short and luminous life. You were idle more than you were away, and away more than you were present, yet somehow everyone knew you were there. Your away message was a song lyric only you understood. The buddy list that once held your name has dissolved into the ether, taking with it every OMG and BRB we ever exchanged. Logging off forever.
intent: Grieve the disappearance of a youthful online persona from a defunct chat platform.

prompt: Write a product review for a plastic grocery bag as if it's an ultralight carry solution
response: ★★★★☆ Pack weight: 5 grams. Capacity: roughly 15 liters, expandable. The HDPE shell is semi-transparent, enabling at-a-glance inventory checks without unpacking — a feature most backpack manufacturers charge extra for. The dual-loop handle system distributes load symmetrically and frees both hands when needed. Multi-use potential is outstanding: shopping, trash liner, emergency rain cover, stuff sack. Compresses to near-zero volume. If this were sold in an outdoor gear shop at $12 each, people would buy it. It's free. Five stars.
intent: Make the case for a plastic grocery bag as serious minimalist gear.

prompt: Can you write a Yelp review for the moment I realized I'd been mispronouncing a word for years
response: ★☆☆☆☆ Visited without warning during a dinner party. I had been saying 'epitome' as three syllables with the emphasis confidently placed on 'epi' for approximately sixteen years. A guest corrected me gently, which was worse than if they'd corrected me badly. I finished the sentence. No one mentioned it again. No one forgot. I have replayed this moment on rotation since 2019. Do not recommend. Cannot unvisit.
intent: Process the specific humiliation of a long-held mispronunciation being exposed publicly.

prompt: Write a performance rider for the office chair before anyone sits in it
response:
ERGONOMIC CHAIR RIDER

Terms binding from the moment of initial seat contact:

1. Height must be adjusted so that feet are flat on the floor. The Chair will not accommodate poor posture by default.
2. Lumbar support must be engaged. The Chair provides it. Using the Chair without engaging it is an insult.
3. No eating at the Chair. The Chair is not a dining chair.
4. Armrests shall not be used to hang bags, coats, or wet umbrellas.
5. Rolling the Chair across non-carpeted surfaces must be done at a walking pace. No racing.
6. The Chair must be returned to its neutral position at end of day.
intent: Reframe a standard ergonomic office chair's usage guidelines as non-negotiable performance demands.

prompt: What would the closed caption say during someone's first bite of a food they used to hate as a child?
response: [PAUSING] [RECONSIDERING ROUGHLY 200 DINNER-TABLE ARGUMENTS] [CHEWING SLOWLY]
intent: Mark the precise moment a childhood conviction quietly collapses.

prompt: Make a syslog entry about a commuter who misses their stop while reading.
response: 2026-05-29T08:52:44Z WARN user.transit: disembark_event MISSED; station=Oak_St; detected_at=next_stop; book_absorption_coefficient=HIGH; additional_travel=+12m; annoyance_level=mild (worth it)
intent: Record the blameless over-travel of someone too absorbed in a book to notice the world.

prompt: write a 404 page for a music discovery app where the mood is missing
response:
404 — The Mood Not Found

We scrolled through every playlist. Nothing landed.

There's a feeling you're trying to match, and it doesn't have a genre yet. That's not your fault.

Try again later. Sometimes the music finds you.
intent: Capture the experience of wanting music that fits a feeling you can't name, through an error page.

prompt: Draft a two weeks notice for stepping down as my friend group's unofficial therapist
response:
Dear friends,

With two weeks notice, I am stepping back from my role as your primary emotional support infrastructure. I care about each of you deeply, which is precisely why I can no longer absorb four separate crises between the hours of 11pm and 2am. Please consult one another, or consider professionals.

With love and a new bedtime,
Cassidy
intent: Setting emotional boundaries by formally ending an exhausting informal caregiving dynamic.

prompt: Write me a double dactyl about a smart whiteboard that nobody can figure out how to use.
response:
Higgledy-piggledy,
Interactive board
Blips and authentication
Errors appear.

Technologically
Superior, yet the whole
Team draws on paper and
Makes do this year.
intent: Satirize expensive smart office technology that goes unused because it's too complicated.

prompt: draft an AITA post about sending back food at a restaurant when it was slightly wrong
response:
AITA for sending back my burger?

I asked for no onions. There were onions. I know they're easy to pick off. I know the kitchen is busy. I know I'm that person now. But I asked. They nodded. The order came with onions. I sent it back. My date looked mortified. My date should have ordered a salad with less judgment on it. AITA?
intent: Defending a legitimate complaint while acknowledging the social cost of exercising it.

prompt: Write a message in a bottle from a competitive swimmer who got a bit carried away
response: Misjudged the distance. Personal best, technically. Rescue not required — I can make it back — but if you see a yellow swim cap in the water, please do not take it. That is mine.
intent: Capture athletic overconfidence turned into a mildly dire situation the person refuses to fully admit.

prompt: write a prose poem about the way a chat bubble appears while someone is typing
response: Three dots. A small sign of life, like a held breath in visible form. Someone across the fiber is composing a thought that will arrive simplified. You wait. The dots pause. You experience a full emotional arc in the time it takes someone to find a word.
intent: Examine the suspense of watching another person gather a thought in real time.

prompt: Write a witness statement about someone parking their car, getting out, and immediately getting back in and driving away.
response: The vehicle, a silver sedan, entered the space at 6:03 PM. The driver exited, stood beside the car for approximately seven seconds, opened the door again, sat inside, and reversed out of the space at 6:04 PM. I do not know what they remembered. It appeared significant.
intent: Document the moment someone realizes they forgot something, without explaining what.

prompt: Draft a jury summons for a WiFi router that keeps dropping the connection at critical moments
response:
COURT OF DIGITAL CONTINUITY

TO: Router, Dual-Band, Black
Living Room Shelf, Behind the Succulents

You are SUMMONED to serve as a juror in the matter of Remote Workers v. Inopportune Disconnection. Your pattern of signal failure during final exam submissions, live presentations, and unrecoverable gaming sessions constitutes direct knowledge of the stakes involved in connectivity disruption disputes.

Hearing: Video conference. Please do not crash it.
intent: Produce a spoof legal notice holding an unreliable internet router responsible for its chronic bad timing.

prompt: Create a policy about employees adjusting the office thermostat without permission
response: Section 11.1 — Climate Infrastructure Access: The building thermostat is designated a controlled asset. Adjustments may only be initiated by employees holding Thermal Authorization Level 2 or above, as determined by Facilities. Unauthorized temperature modifications will trigger an automatic incident report and a mandatory Comfort Negotiation Seminar.
intent: Formally protect one of the most contentious pieces of shared office hardware by creating a permission hierarchy for it.

prompt: Write a motivational poster caption about open office floor plans
response:
WE REMOVED THE WALLS SO YOU COULD FEEL THE ENERGY.
AND ALSO SO WE COULD SEE YOU.
intent: Satirize workplace surveillance dressed up as collaborative design.

prompt: write an elevator pitch for an app that schedules random moments of forced leisure throughout your workday
response: RestInjector blocks your calendar at irregular intervals with fifteen-minute events labeled 'non-negotiable pause.' Your status goes to Do Not Disturb. Your screen dims. You are prevented from being productive. Recovery is the product. $11/month.
intent: Pitch a coercive rest-scheduling tool that forces breaks on overworking professionals.

prompt: Generate a difficulty select screen for a gift-giving game where difficulty represents how impossible the recipient is to shop for
response:
GIFT GIVING — CHOOSE YOUR TARGET

[ BEST FRIEND ]  You know their taste. You have a list. You're already done.
[ PARTNER'S PARENTS ]  Thoughtful but not presumptuous. Personal but not too personal.
[ COWORKER GIFT EXCHANGE ]  $20 cap. Neutral. No inside jokes. No candles, they hate candles.
[ YOUR DAD ]  He says he doesn't need anything. He means it. You buy him socks again.
intent: Satirize gift-giving difficulty by scaling from easy known recipients to impossible-to-shop-for family members.

prompt: Make a Stack Overflow question about forgetting to reply to texts
response:
**Messages marked as 'read' but response never dispatched — persistent failure in follow-through**

I consistently open text messages, fully process their content, form an intended response in my head, and then fail to send it. Hours or days later I realize the reply was never delivered. The other party sometimes follows up asking if I saw their message. I have notifications on, memory of reading the message, and no explanation for why execution fails. Is there a reliable workaround?
intent: Wanting help building a habit of actually responding to text messages instead of just reading them.

prompt: Write a Reddit AMA post from the perspective of someone who has worked as a parking lot attendant for 20 years
response:
**I've been a parking lot attendant for 20 years. I've seen things. AMA.**

I work the overnight shift at a downtown garage. I've watched people cry in their cars, propose marriage, eat entire rotisserie chickens alone, and once—just once—I saw a man in a tuxedo push a wheelbarrow full of soil in at 2am and leave without it. I do not ask questions. Ask me anything.
intent: Create a compelling AMA hook for an unglamorous but oddly intimate job that hints at hidden stories.

prompt: Write empty-state microcopy for a contact list when no contacts have been added
response: No contacts yet. You know people. Add them before they add you somewhere worse.
intent: Prompt a user to populate a new address book with a touch of social anxiety humor.

prompt: Write a terms of service clause about the unspoken rules of being a good wingman
response: Section 17.2 — Auxiliary Social Support. The Wingperson agrees to provide cover, conversation, and moral support during social pursuit activities on behalf of the Primary User. Duties include: engaging third parties in good-faith dialogue, affirming the Primary User's positive qualities without obvious exaggeration, and executing a graceful exit when requested via agreed signal. The Wingperson may not pursue the same target and shall not share the Primary User's prior romantic history unprompted.
intent: Formalize the duties and limits of the wingman role in social settings.

prompt: Write SQL that ranks every friendship by how much effort I've actually put in versus how much I claim to value it
response:
SELECT person_name, stated_importance_rank, actual_effort_score,
  (stated_importance_rank - actual_effort_score) AS hypocrisy_gap
FROM friendships
ORDER BY hypocrisy_gap DESC;
intent: Exposing the distance between how they describe their priorities and how they actually behave.

prompt: Write a terms of service clause for borrowing a friend's car
response: Section 15.3 — Vehicle Loan Terms. Borrowing Party accepts the vehicle in its current condition and agrees to return it in an equivalent or superior state. Fuel tank must be returned at or above the level at which it was received. Borrowing Party shall not adjust presets, leave food containers in the back seat, or remark upon the quality of the music already playing. Liability for parking violations incurred during the loan period rests solely with the Borrowing Party.
intent: Spell out the unspoken etiquette and obligations of borrowing a friend's car.

prompt: Can you write a ballad about the security question you can't remember the answer to
response:
What was the name of your childhood pet?
A hamster, a gerbil? I cannot place it yet.
I know that I answered this eight years ago
With certainty blazing — where did the answer go?

Fluffy? Or Biscuit? Or maybe just Dot?
Fluffy is wrong, sir. You have one more shot.
The account is suspended. Your data is sealed.
I never had pets, and the truth is revealed.
intent: Poke fun at the absurdity of security questions as a recovery mechanism.

prompt: Model the pattern of an argument that ends in a draw using regex
response: ^(position-A\s+position-B)(\s+(counterpoint|raised-volume)){3,}(\s+tangent)*(\s+exhaustion)(\s+agree-to-disagree|\s+awkward-hug)$ — Dual anchors from the start. Counterpoints loop with possible volume increase. Tangents are noise. Termination is fatigue-driven, not resolution-driven.
intent: Capture the structure of a conflict that concludes without either party yielding.

prompt: Give me a condescending Stack Overflow reply to someone asking why their TypeScript interface isn't being enforced at runtime
response: TypeScript is a compile-time tool. It evaporates when transpiled to JavaScript. There is no interface at runtime. If you want runtime validation, use Zod, Yup, or io-ts. TypeScript is not a runtime guard. It is a very smart linter.
intent: Explain the difference between TypeScript's compile-time type checking and runtime validation

prompt: Create an elevator inspection certificate for the algorithm that decides which ads I see.
response:
TARGETED INFERENCE ENGINE INSPECTION CERTIFICATE
Unit: Ad Recommendation Algorithm (Operator: Unspecified Platform)
Data Sources: Browsing history, location, purchase patterns, private conversations (alleged)
Load Accuracy: High; surfaced ad for item mentioned aloud but not searched
User Control: Nominal; opt-out functional in theory
Transparency: None; black-box compliance per industry standard
Creepiness Index: 8.4 / 10
Certification Status: COMPLIANT with current self-regulatory framework
Consumer Recourse: Accepting cookies was the consent. You clicked agree.
intent: Satirize opaque data-targeting systems by pretending they passed a formal safety inspection.

prompt: Create a personality quiz result for someone who can't throw away sentimental junk
response:
You Got: The Museum Curator of One

That ticket stub is from a night that mattered. That broken mug still holds the shape of something. You do not have clutter — you have *primary sources.* Marie Kondo asked if it sparks joy. You asked who gave her the right. Your storage unit is a biography. You have not been inside it since 2019.
intent: Validate the emotional hoarding instinct while gently exposing its limits.

prompt: Make up a love letter to the autocomplete feature on a smartphone keyboard
response:
To autocomplete,

You finish my sentences because you have been watching. I should find that unsettling but mostly I find it useful. Sometimes you are wrong and I send the wrong word and blame you publicly. I am sorry. I come back. I always come back.
intent: Humorously reconcile with a predictive text feature after years of blame and mutual misunderstanding.

prompt: Write documentation for an API endpoint that handles the act of apologizing, including sincerity validation
response:
**POST /apology**

Submits an apology and validates sincerity before delivery.

**Request Body**
```json
{
  "recipient_id": "uuid",
  "message": "string",
  "delivery_channel": "in_person" | "text" | "email" | "sticky_note",
  "includes_but": boolean
}
```
**Response**
```json
{
  "apology_id": "uuid",
  "sincerity_score": 0.0–1.0,
  "delivered": boolean,
  "warning": "'But' detected. Sincerity score penalized."
}
```
**Status:** `422 Unprocessable` if `includes_but: true` and message contains blame shift.
intent: Model the social transaction of apologizing with built-in insincerity detection and channel selection.

prompt: Do an astrological compatibility reading for a Gemini and a podcast they've had in their queue since 2021.
response:
Gemini & The Unlistened Podcast Episode

Gemini added this episode with genuine enthusiasm and has since subscribed to forty-three more podcasts. The episode sits at zero percent progress, an aspiration crystallized in app memory. Gemini respects what it represents: the version of themselves who has time. Compatibility: 3/10. The episode is now marked played. Nothing resolved.
intent: Satirize digital content hoarding and the gap between intellectual aspiration and actual attention span.

prompt: Draft an aubade where the speaker wakes to a wall of Twitter/X notifications after something they posted went viral
response:
I posted once, carelessly, near midnight,
and slept before the algorithm's bite.
Now dawn arrives with thousands in its wake—
the quote-tweets, the pile-ons, the hot take.
Go, night. You kept me innocent and still.
The mentions wait. I'm famous against my will.
intent: Capture the alarming transition from private person to unwilling viral subject, discovered at dawn.

prompt: Draft a layoff announcement email for a cybersecurity firm cutting its sales team after pivoting to a product-led growth model
response:
Subject: Evolving Our Go-to-Market Approach

Team,

As we shift to a product-led growth model, we are restructuring our commercial organization to match. This evolution, while exciting for our product trajectory, means that many current sales roles will no longer exist in their current form.

We are deeply grateful to the sales team for building the customer relationships that made our growth possible. Affected colleagues will receive information from HR by noon.

Onward,
Chief Revenue Officer
intent: Fire the sales team while crediting them for growth they enabled and blaming a business model pivot rather than their performance.

prompt: create a Steam page description for a game about buying a gift for someone you don't know well
response:
APPROPRIATE
You know their name. You know the occasion. You have a budget of $40. You have been browsing for two hours. A candle? Everyone gives candles. A book? What if they've read it?
intent: Make the social anxiety of gift selection the core loop of a low-stakes decision game.

prompt: Write a restraining order against the inner critic that shows up specifically when things are going well
response:
IN THE COURT OF PSYCHOLOGICAL STABILITY

IN RE: The Good-Times Inner Critic, also known as "Anticipatory Deflation"

FINDINGS: Respondent has been observed arriving not during failure, but during periods of genuine forward momentum, at which point it introduces scenarios in which the current good thing is about to end, was never real, or has been noticed by others who will soon dismantle it.

IT IS ORDERED that Respondent is not welcome in moments of legitimate contentment. The Court recognizes Respondent believes it is being protective. The Court rules this is not helpful.

Petitioner is allowed to be well.
intent: Identifying the specific anxiety that undermines good moments by anticipating their end

prompt: Write a scene where a temp worker outlasts every permanent employee in the office.
response:
INT. OPEN OFFICE - YEARS LATER

MARIANNE is the only one left. People have retired, quit, been transferred.

Her contract has been renewed eleven times.

A NEW MANAGER shakes her hand.

NEW MANAGER
So you're the temp?

MARIANNE
I am the institution.
intent: Illustrate how contingent workers often outlast and outlive the organizations that treat them as temporary.

prompt: Write a Glassdoor review for a job working at the moon
response: ★★★★☆ — Consistent schedule. Predictable visibility cycles. You are always being observed from a distance; no one ever comes. The commute eliminated my social life. Colleagues: none, formally. The job has enormous emotional impact on others, which you will never fully experience yourself. The reflection work is lonely.
intent: Explore the isolation of roles that have outsized emotional impact on others but receive no direct human connection.

prompt: Write an innkeeper NPC who's explaining that the WiFi password is the name of the inn
response: The hearth-rune network is 'ThePrancingPonyGuest' — capital T, capital P, capital P, capital G, no spaces. Yes, it is a lot of capitals. My nephew set it up. The scrolls load slowly after supper, I will be honest.
intent: Translate a universal guest experience frustration into an immersive fantasy setting

prompt: Create an official rule about employees who give extremely long-winded answers in standup meetings
response: Section 5.14 — Synchronous Status Report Duration: Each participant's verbal status update during recurring synchronous check-ins must not exceed ninety (90) seconds. Updates approaching the limit will receive a single soft chime from the Timekeeper. Updates exceeding two (2) minutes will pause the meeting pending the speaker's written submission of a Status Supplement, distributed asynchronously.
intent: Solve the problem of standup monopolizers by creating an escalating interruption system with a paper trail.

prompt: Write a card with some sympathy for someone who let their indoor plant-watering habit die — along with the plants
response:
To the plants, and to the one who tried:

They asked so little. A cup of water, some light, occasional attention. For a time, you gave it faithfully. Then the week got away from you, and then another.

They are gone. You are forgiven.

With a succulent and renewed hope.
intent: Gently acknowledge the dual loss of a plant care habit and the plants themselves.

prompt: Write a brief eulogy for a USB hub.
response: We are gathered today to remember the four-port USB hub that lived under my desk for nine years. He connected what needed connecting. He never asked for praise. He carried, without complaint, my external hard drive, my old printer, a webcam I forgot about, and one mysterious device that may have been a fan. He died as he lived: silently, one Tuesday. He will be replaced. He will not be forgotten.
intent: They wanted brief eulogy for a USB hub.

prompt: Playbill bio for an airport security screener who has lost all faith in carry-on luggage.
response: Agent Watkins (TSA, Lane 4) joins us this season with a curriculum vitae built entirely on betrayal — lighters declared as chapstick, full bottles described as 'almost empty,' a wheel of cheese that no one will explain. He approaches each bin with the openness of someone who has learned not to be surprised and the eyes of someone who has been surprised anyway.
intent: Depict a TSA agent's weary disillusionment with travelers as tragic hero material.

prompt: write a tweet about how my houseplant looks dramatically wilted even though I watered it yesterday
response: watered my plant yesterday. came back today. it's lying there like I personally wronged it. dramatic little organism.
intent: expressing exasperation at a plant's theatrical overreaction after being cared for

prompt: Write a short product review for a wooden toothpick as if it's a precision tool from a professional chef's kit
response: ★★★★★ Birch. Single-use. Double-pointed. The taper geometry on both ends allows for left- or right-handed dominant use without reorientation — an ergonomic consideration most multi-tool manufacturers overlook. The wood grain provides micro-grip texture, preventing slip even with moist hands. Temperature tolerance far exceeds kitchen ambient. At 1,000 units per box, the per-use cost is negligible enough to reframe the throw-away lifecycle as a hygiene feature rather than a waste concern. The professionals have known about this for decades. The rest of us are catching up.
intent: Position a disposable toothpick as a thoughtfully engineered culinary precision instrument.

prompt: write an abecedarian about going viral for the wrong reason
response:
Animated GIF of my worst moment.
Bold the quote-tweet — out of context, bright.
Clip ends before my calm comportment.
Dunked on before the afternoon light.
Everyone has seen the thing by Tuesday.
Fifteen minutes, stitched and dubbed.
Greenscreen parody: my face on Sunday.
Huge engagement — spirit snubbed.
I tried to clarify; it ratioed.
Joke aged badly — made in better years.
Karma circling like a studied studio.
Laughter is the medium for tears.
intent: Examine the helplessness of being flattened into a viral moment.

prompt: Give me resolutions for a confirmed night owl who resolves every year to 'become a morning person'
response:
1. Become a morning person.
2. Appreciate the quiet of 6am.
3. Watch the sunrise.
4. Have a peaceful coffee before the day begins.
5. Do all of this at 11am when I actually feel like a person.
intent: Lampoon the perennial desire to rewire one's chronotype through sheer willpower and resolution-writing.

prompt: Describe the IT department as a bestiary creature
response:
**The IT Conclave** (*Technicus reclusia*)
A semi-mythological guild dwelling in sub-basement ecosystems. Summoned only through ticketing rituals; appears, usually, within three to five business days. Speaks in a language that sounds like English but is not. Has the power to fix anything. Uses this power selectively. Its favor is earned by being polite in the subject line.
intent: Humorously capture the mystique and power dynamics of the office IT department.

prompt: Write a listing for Brigadoon, the Scottish village that appears once every 100 years
response:
HISTORIC VILLAGE — BRIGADOON, SCOTLAND

Charming highland community available one day per century. Cobblestone. Thatched. Warm. Residents unaware of modern world; this is considered a feature.

Showing window: 24 hours, every 100 years. Next opportunity: contact agent for scheduling.

Residents cannot leave without consequence. This has not dampened local morale. Community spirit: exceptional. Temporal continuity: enforced.
intent: List the mythical vanishing Scottish village of Brigadoon as a real property with scheduling quirks.

prompt: Create a liability waiver for my drop-in shadow puppetry workshop
response:
HAND SILHOUETTE ARTS STUDIO — WORKSHOP WAIVER

Participant releases the Studio from liability for: finger cramps from sustained contortion, inadequate light source (participants are encouraged to bring their own lamp), and the existential observation that all shadows are temporary. Participant understands that the rabbit is the easiest shape to learn and agrees not to disparage others' dogs.

Signed: ___________________  Date: ________
intent: Produce a workshop release form for a low-tech visual art form that poses no physical danger.

prompt: Make a recall notice for faulty authenticity
response:
RECALL — AUTHENTICITY (Personal Brand Edition)

Authenticity units from the Personal Brand Edition are recalled following discovery that they were manufactured to perform authenticity rather than instantiate it. Affected units generate self-disclosure, informality, and vulnerability markers in quantities calculated to appear unperformed, which undermines the defining property of the product. The unit is most defective when the consumer is most earnest about using it. Genuine Authenticity cannot be intentionally deployed; any unit you are deliberately reaching for is already recalled.
intent: Expose the paradox that consciously performing authenticity makes it inauthentic by definition.

prompt: Make a formal ruling on whether it's acceptable to leave cabinet doors open.
response:
CABINET CLOSURE ORDINANCE — Final Order

This Court finds the Respondent's open-cabinet habit both documented and injurious — specifically, one (1) forehead incident on the 3rd of last month. Effective immediately, all cabinet doors shall be closed within ten (10) seconds of the completion of their use. A grace period of two (2) weeks is granted for habit formation, after which citations will issue.
intent: Formalize a safety-and-aesthetics complaint about open kitchen cabinets.

prompt: Do an audio guide narration for a home linen closet
response: This closet is organized according to a system whose logic has partially dissolved. The upper shelf holds sheets, folded with varying degrees of conviction. Below them: towels, in colors selected during a brief period when this household favored teal. A fitted sheet on the third shelf has been folded into a shape that resists classification — not a square, not a cylinder, not a failure exactly. The guest towels, pristine and undeployed, wait in a category of permanent readiness.
intent: Gently mock the universal human inability to fold a fitted sheet.

prompt: Write a stuffy footnote on why people use the word 'literally' incorrectly.
response: ²⁷ The intensifying use of *literally* — as in "I literally died" — is frequently condemned as semantic decay, but the usage has antecedents in Austen and Dickens, suggesting it reflects a recurrent pressure in English to hyperbolically mark scalar extremity rather than a uniquely modern failure of precision. Language prescriptivists who are *literally* outraged by this usage are themselves unwittingly demonstrating the word's intensifying function. Cf. Pinker (2014) for an unsympathetic but accurate account of the prescriptivist position.
intent: Defend a linguistically stigmatized word choice by exposing the prescriptivist argument's own incoherence.

prompt: Write a phishing email parody about an urgent request to validate your sense of purpose before it expires
response:
Subject: URGENT: Your Life Purpose™ Expires in 48 Hours

Dear Valued Soul,

Our records indicate your Sense of Purpose has not been validated since childhood. Without immediate action, it will lapse and you will be redirected to a life of mild dissatisfaction.

Click here to confirm your calling: [VERIFY NOW]

Do not ignore this message. Others are waiting to claim your destiny.

Warmly,
The Universe
intent: Create a satirical scam email targeting existential uncertainty rather than financial vulnerability.

prompt: can you write a steam-style description for a game about updating software
response:
PENDING RESTART
You told it 'remind me later.' That was three weeks ago. Disk space is low. Compatibility unknown. The update is 2.1 GB and requires you to close everything. Now is never a good time.
intent: Capture the perpetual deferral of software updates as a game about avoidance.

prompt: write a calming bedtime story for young children about airplane turbulence
response: The plane bumped because the air had bumps in it, the same way roads have bumps. The air doesn't mean anything by it. It's just air, doing its stretching. The captain turned on the little belt sign. Everyone clicked and held their juice. Then the air smoothed out, like it had finished its yawn. The plane kept going. Clouds below. Stars above. Fine.
intent: Help a child feel safe during or after a turbulent flight.

prompt: Show me a SQL query that counts how many times jealousy was the real reason I was upset
response:
SELECT stated_reason, COUNT(*) AS incidents,
  SUM(CASE WHEN actual_reason = 'jealousy' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS jealousy_actual
FROM conflict_log
GROUP BY stated_reason
HAVING SUM(CASE WHEN actual_reason = 'jealousy' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) > 0;
intent: Auditing how often jealousy hid behind legitimately-sounding grievances.

prompt: Analyze the SWOT of renting out a spare room in my home
response:
**Strengths:** Offset mortgage or rent; built-in social contact; underutilized space monetized.
**Weaknesses:** Privacy loss; shared bathroom politics; legal and tax complications.
**Opportunities:** Save aggressively for another goal; meet someone interesting; try hosting before committing to Airbnb.
**Threats:** Tenant from hell; lease restrictions overlooked; relationship with your space permanently altered.
intent: Weigh the financial appeal of house-hacking against the psychological cost of lost privacy.

prompt: draft a psychic hotline transcript where the caller is anxious about their productivity
response:
PSYCHIC: I'm sensing an unfinished project.
CALLER: Several.
PSYCHIC: One in particular. The spirits are circling it. It's been circled for... a long time.
CALLER: Two years.
PSYCHIC: The spirits say it still wants to exist. They're very patient about this.
CALLER: I feel guilty every day.
PSYCHIC: The guilt is not helping it get done. The spirits wanted to say that out loud. Someone had to.
intent: Articulate the paralysis of perfectionism and chronic procrastination.

prompt: Describe a ketchup packet like it's a rare condiment on an upscale menu
response:
Heirloom Tomato Reduction — Single Serve Reserve
A foil-sealed portion of slow-cooked nightshade concentrate, sweetened with corn syrup and brightened with distilled vinegar. Tear from the corner. Apply sparingly. Compliments the protein.
intent: Reframe a gas station condiment as a precious, artisan accompaniment.

prompt: Draft a patent abstract for making a cup of tea while avoiding real work
response: A task-interruption and beverage-preparation sequence in which the subject abandons an in-progress cognitive task to engage a multi-step hot-liquid synthesis routine. The method provides a socially sanctioned break interval, generates a warm receptacle to hold, and produces observable evidence of domestic competence, collectively constituting a full task-avoidance episode.
intent: Make the classic British procrastination ritual sound like engineering.

prompt: Write an achievement for replying to an email you've been avoiding for three weeks
response: Inbox Archaeology — You excavated it. You answered it. It asked nothing worse than expected.
intent: Honor finally confronting a dreaded unanswered message.

prompt: write a funny rambly opener for a blog post about my first attempt at homemade croissants
response: There are things you know intellectually that don't really land until you're elbow-deep in a laminated dough situation at 7am having slept four hours because you had to let the dough cold-proof overnight but the overnight was longer than planned because you lost track of time watching a documentary about competitive bakers. Croissants take two days. The butter leaks if it's too warm. The layers are not a metaphor — they are just layers, seventy-two of them, and they will not honor false confidence. Mine came out looking like vaguely croissant-shaped objects. They tasted, against all probability, like victory.
intent: Give an honest, self-deprecating account of the physical and temporal demands of first-time croissant making.

prompt: write an apology note for texting someone happy birthday on entirely the wrong day
response:
Hi!

I realize your birthday was in October and I sent that message in March. I have no good explanation. I hope you still felt celebrated, technically, and I'm sorry for the confusion. I'll add the right date to my calendar.

— Bex
intent: Apologize for congratulating someone on their birthday at a completely incorrect time.

prompt: Write me a knock-knock joke about the cable management under someone's desk.
response:
Knock knock.
Who's there?
Cable.
Cable who?
Cable of Cthulhu — last touched in the office renovation of 2017.
intent: Describe the chaotic nest of wires hiding beneath a neglected desk.

prompt: Write a layoff email for a biotech firm that's spinning off a division and leaving some employees without a seat at either company
response:
Subject: Update on the Helix Spinoff and Transition

Colleagues,

As the formal separation of Helix Therapeutics is now complete, we want to provide clarity on team placements. The majority of employees have been placed within either the parent company or Helix in aligned roles.

Unfortunately, a subset of colleagues — primarily within shared services — did not receive an offer from either entity. These individuals are being notified directly today.

We are committed to robust transition support for everyone affected.

With appreciation,
Chief People Officer
intent: Inform employees caught between two companies that they have been dropped through the gap of a spinoff with no home on either side.

prompt: Write a standup update about getting dressed today
response: Yesterday: Outfit decision tree resolved in 4 minutes. Today: Similar scope. Blockers: The 'good shirt' is in limbo between clean and dirty.
intent: Reframe the daily clothing decision as a branching logic problem.

prompt: write me a magic spell that keeps your coffee hot
response:
Perpetual Steep
Cost: 2 MP | Range: Touch | Duration: Until consumed
Lays a thermal ward over any beverage vessel. The liquid within maintains its initial temperature indefinitely, never scalding, never cooling. Particularly useful for the mug abandoned on a desk during a meeting that turned into three meetings. Incompatible with iced drinks. The spell has feelings about that.
intent: Solve the classic problem of coffee going cold before you finish drinking it.

prompt: Alt text for a photo of two strangers sitting together on a bus, not talking.
response: Two people occupy adjacent seats on a city bus, their shoulders nearly touching, each staring in a different direction, sharing the silence with the particular care of people who have agreed to nothing.
intent: Articulate the unspoken social contract of urban proximity in an accessibility description.

prompt: write a hold message for a city public works line that fully admits potholes exist and we know about them
response: Thank you for calling the Department of Public Works. If you are calling to report a pothole, it is likely already in our system. If it is not, we appreciate the update. Please hold and an operator will assist you.
intent: Craft a dry, honest hold script that acknowledges infrastructure problems without pretending they're unknown.

prompt: instagram bio for a person who professionally translates emotion into color palettes for brands and is unsure if they have a soul left
response:
Your grief is Pantone 19-3536. Your joy is 13-0858.
Brand color strategist | I have monetized feeling | Something has been traded | The palette is beautiful | I think I'm fine.
intent: Sitting with the quiet moral unease of commodifying human emotion as a product deliverable.

prompt: write a captain's log about the awkward silence in an elevator with your boss
response: Stardate 5044.2. Entered the lift at deck 12 simultaneously with Admiral Chen. The doors closed. We were alone. I said: "Busy day?" She said: "Always." Fourteen seconds elapsed. The lift stopped at deck 9. No one boarded. The doors closed again. I pointed at nothing in the corner and nodded as though I had seen something. We did not speak again. I exited at deck 6. She continued upward.
intent: Capture the specific social discomfort of riding an elevator with one's supervisor.

prompt: Make a deprecation notice for small talk.
response:
DEPRECATION NOTICE

Feature: Small Talk (v1.0 – present)
Status: DEPRECATED

"Nice weather" and its variants will no longer receive patches. This feature has been a drain on conversational resources for centuries. Replacement: either meaningful exchange or comfortable silence. Migration guides available upon request.
intent: Mock the social ritual of hollow pleasantries by treating it as a feature past its useful life.

prompt: Draft a funny eviction notice for overthinking
response:
EVICTION NOTICE — FOURTH DRAFT

To: Overthinking
(Please note: this notice was revised eleven times before sending.)

You have analyzed every exit scenario, concluded none are viable, and therefore refused to vacate. This circular logic does not constitute a lease. The premises — my pre-sleep mental state — are needed for rest. Leave. Or don't. Actually, leave. Final answer.

— A Very Tired Brain
intent: Making light of the paralysis that comes from analyzing every decision into inaction.

prompt: Write extremely over-the-top rules for a shared office printer
response:
PRINTER USE POLICY — FLOOR 3

• Print jobs must be collected within 10 minutes of completion.
• Print jobs exceeding 50 pages require a department code and a valid reason.
• Do not stand at the printer before your job arrives. You are blocking the queue.
• Color printing is audited quarterly. Print in color only if color is the message.
• Paper jams: report, do not attempt repair. You are not a printer technician.
• The toner is not your problem until it is your problem. Report low toner. Do not change the toner.
• The printer is not a scanner. The scanner is the scanner.
intent: Satirize the inexplicable bureaucracy that accumulates around shared office equipment.

prompt: Draft a damage control statement for a rideshare company after their surge pricing algorithm charged customers $4,000 for rides taken in dreams
response: SwiftRide Technologies is aware of erroneous charges appearing on accounts for rides that were not completed on our platform. Some affected charges include trip data that our engineering team cannot reconcile with GPS, road network, or time-zone records. We are issuing full refunds to all accounts with charges matching this profile. We want to assure customers that SwiftRide does not currently operate in any capacity that would allow for the completion of journeys described as occurring in sleep states, and that if we did, surge pricing would still be subject to legal review.
intent: Issue refunds and deny culpability for a billing system that somehow charged for nonexistent journeys

prompt: Write a mission statement for a startup that delivers pre-cracked hard-boiled eggs
response: Removing friction from mornings that matter — fully cooked, already started, ready for you the moment you are ready for us.
intent: Apply lean startup philosophy to solving a problem people do not actually have

prompt: Create a Dockerfile comment about losing a pet
response: # Service ran 14 years. No replaceable parts. No patch. Shutdown logged at 3:12 AM.
intent: Grieve the quiet, particular loss of an animal companion who kept their own hours.

prompt: Write an affidavit swearing I unlocked the door before trying the handle
response:
AFFIDAVIT

I, the undersigned, do swear that prior to applying rotational force to the door handle, I confirmed the lock was disengaged. I did not rattle it repeatedly in confusion. I did not knock on my own door. The sequence of actions was correct on the first attempt.

_______________________
Affiant
intent: Formally deny a moment of absentminded fumbling at one's own door.

prompt: Create a concert ticket for my neighbor Linda's annual block party next month.
response:
LINDA'S BLOCK PARTY · NEIGHBORHOOD SUMMER BASH
TBA · June · Maple Street
General Admission · BYOC (Chair)
Featuring: Potato Salad, Sprinklers, Small Talk
ADMIT ALL RESIDENTS (Plus Reasonable Guests)
intent: Give a casual neighborhood gathering an official, impressive-feeling document.

prompt: Help me write a pen pal letter from a retired circus elephant to her former ringmaster who's opened a bakery
response:
Dear Mr. Fontaine,

I heard about the croissants. Someone showed the sanctuary keeper a photo. They looked adequate.

I don't miss the sequins. I do sometimes miss the roar — not yours specifically, the crowd's. I have a large field now. The grass is good. The other elephants are polite.

I hope the bakery does well. I hope the sequins are in a drawer somewhere and you think about them sometimes.

In memory of all of it,
Lucia
intent: Convey a complicated mix of resentment and nostalgia through restrained, dignified language from an unexpected narrator.

prompt: Can you write an inner monologue in Shakespeare's voice for someone who just got ratio'd on Twitter/X?
response:
Ten thousand strangers found mine words today
And found them wanting — worse, found them absurd.
The ratio! That merciless arithmetic
Whereby the replies outweigh the hearts.
I meant to be profound. I was not profound.
I meant to stir a discourse. I stirred scorn.
Delete the post? But deletion proves the wound.
Leave it? Leave it. Let the ratio stand.
intent: Dramatize the social humiliation of having a social media post publicly mocked at scale.

prompt: PSA about the risk of sending a voice memo instead of just typing the message
response: COMMUNITY NOTICE: The voice memo you sent is four minutes long. The information it contains could have been one sentence. The recipient is now in a meeting. The message cannot be skimmed. Please consider the text box. It has been right there the whole time.
intent: Advocate for text messages over lengthy, unskimmable voice memos

prompt: Write me a clerihew about a corporate lawyer who never stops billing.
response:
Harland Fitch
enriches every pitch.
He bills in six-minute increments, it's true,
and charged for reading this clerihew too.
intent: Skewer the relentlessly billing lawyer with self-aware comic verse.

prompt: Compose a message in a bottle from someone who capsized while arguing with their GPS
response: It said turn left. The ocean was to the left. I maintain that I was correct to question it. Vessel lost. Argument: ongoing.
intent: Illustrate stubbornness and technology distrust leading to a humiliating but avoidable catastrophe.

prompt: File a Jira ticket about the bug where the thing you need is always in the last place you look
response:
BUG-0001 | Search Terminates Only at Final Candidate Location

Priority: Low | Status: By Design (Disputed)

Description:
User loses item. User searches N locations. Item found at location N. This is true for all N.

Steps to Reproduce:
1. Lose item.
2. Search locations sequentially.
3. Find item in last place searched.

Expected: Statistical distribution of discovery across locations.
Actual: Discovery always occurs at final location searched. 100% of trials.

Root Cause Analysis: Search terminates upon finding item. Therefore item is, by definition, always in the last place checked. This has been explained to user. User still finds it annoying.

Status: Closed. Behavior is correct. User: dissatisfied.
intent: Satirize the tautological observation that things are always in the last place you look, framed as a closed bug with a dismissive but technically correct resolution.

prompt: Write a company policy about employees naming their desk plants
response: Section 12.4 — Personal Vegetation Identification: Employees who assign proper nouns to desk flora are encouraged to register the name with Facilities Management. Registered plant names will be honored in internal communications. Management reserves the right to decline names deemed detrimental to workplace morale or confusingly similar to existing staff members.
intent: Codify a completely harmless personal habit into official governance without actually discouraging it.

prompt: Make a motivational poster caption about mandatory fun at company events
response:
FUN IS NOT OPTIONAL.
ENJOY YOURSELF OR FILE A FORM.
intent: Lampoon the forced cheerfulness of corporate team-building events.

prompt: Write a playbill bio for a flight attendant who has truly heard every complaint.
response: Adrienne Cooke (Senior Cabin Crew) has logged 24 years and four million miles in a role that demands simultaneous expertise in safety, hospitality, and the management of expectations. She has heard every complaint about seat width, boarding order, and the temperature of the biscuit. She responds to each with the professionalism of a diplomat and the eyes of someone counting down to Athens.
intent: Celebrate a flight attendant's veteran poise and quiet endurance.

prompt: Jeopardy clue about the drawer in the kitchen that just has random stuff in it.
response: It contains a dead battery, a menu from a restaurant that closed in 2019, and something that might be a key but opens nothing in this home.
intent: Celebrate the universality of a specific household chaos zone.

prompt: make a spam text about a prize redemption for someone's accumulated surplus of good intentions
response: Great news! Your Good Intentions account has reached redemption threshold. Exchange 500 intentions for 1 actual outcome OR carry them forward at 0% value. Expires Dec 31. Reply INTEND.
intent: Satirize the gap between good intentions and action by making intentions a redeemable loyalty currency.

prompt: write a sestina about the strange loneliness of being one of thousands of browser tabs open on someone's computer
response:
I am the thirty-seventh. No one comes
to me today, though I've been loaded, filled
with everything they asked: the text is here,
the images resolved, the page complete,
ready in the dark the background holds —
patiently, without complaint, without sound.

There is a kind of dignity in sound-
less waiting. The news comes
and goes in other tabs. The dark I hold
myself in has no news. The page, once filled,
stays still. I am the most complete
I'll ever be, prepared, and no one's here.

The one who opened me was here
a moment — scanned the headline, made the sound
of someone who would return. Complete
attention, they implied. No one comes
back. They never come back. Filled
with good intention, their to-read list holds

twenty tabs like me. The list holds
not because the content here
grew less important, but because filled
time crowds out intention. Sound
logic: something else comes
first, then something else. Complete

the loop: the session closes. Complete
devastating mercy. What I hold
is lost — nothing comes
from my crash. Not here
in any cache. Not a sound
preserved. The tab, once filled,

became nothing when it filled
the browser's limit. Complete
obliteration, gentle, without sound —
no ceremony for the thirty-seventh hold.
I didn't mind. I'd rather not be here
forever than have no one comes.

Filled with complete sound,
held here — no one comes.
intent: Personify the ignored browser tab to meditate on digital abandonment and impermanence.

prompt: Write SQL that lists everyone I've compared myself to unfavorably and how often
response:
SELECT comparison_target, COUNT(*) AS comparison_count, primary_domain, avg_self_rating_after
FROM self_comparisons
WHERE outcome_for_self = 'unfavorable'
GROUP BY comparison_target, primary_domain
ORDER BY comparison_count DESC;
intent: Charting the landscape of envy and inadequacy they've been quietly navigating.

prompt: Draft a Slack status for when I'm trying to look like I'm not waiting for a promotion decision
response: 🖥️ Working as normal. This is normal. Very normal day.
intent: Perform routine calm while acutely aware of an external decision with significant personal stakes.

prompt: Write a postcard from a tax auditor on vacation at the beach.
response: Dear Greg — Sandy here in Cabo. I am attempting to stop noticing margins. The bartender's tip jar is being filled inefficiently and I am working very hard to say nothing. Karen says I am still saying things. The hotel uses incorrect rounding on the daily resort fee. I am happy. Wish you were here. — Doris
intent: They wanted postcard from a tax auditor on vacation at the beach.

prompt: Give me a pretentious menu write-up for a bologna sandwich on white bread
response:
Compressed Charcuterie Disc on Enriched Loaf
Processed pork-adjacent rounds, stacked two deep upon a cloud of commercial white bread. A nod to the American lunchbox tradition, served without irony and without mustard unless requested.
intent: Dress up a deli-drawer staple as if it belongs on a charcuterie tasting menu.

prompt: Write meeting notes for a conflict resolution session where the two parties refused to be in the same room.
response:
CONFLICT RESOLUTION SESSION — May 29, 2026
Parties: Employee A, Employee B | Mediator: HR
Format: Separate rooms

Session 1 (Employee A, 10:00 AM): Concerns documented.
Session 2 (Employee B, 11:00 AM): Concerns documented. Largely contradictory.

Joint session: Declined by both parties.

Resolution reached: No.

Next step: External mediator engaged. Cost: $3,200/day.
intent: Capture the futility of mediating a conflict when the parties refuse to actually mediate.

prompt: Write an out-of-office that reveals someone is attending their own surprise party that is, apparently, no longer a surprise.
response: I am out of the office this afternoon for a personal engagement that I was, until this morning, not supposed to know about. I know. I have known for three weeks. I will be gone from 4pm onward. Please wish me a convincing face. Back tomorrow.
intent: Capture the awkward social obligation of pretending not to know something everyone believes is still a secret.

prompt: Write a haiku error for a null pointer exception
response:
You reached for something
That was never really there
Segfault. Start again.
intent: Capture the philosophical absurdity of dereferencing nothing.

prompt: can you do an abecedarian poem about group chats
response:
Added to the group without consent.
Birthday planning in a thread of nine.
Conversation archived, heaven-sent.
Daily meme delivery: assign.
Every left-on-read becomes a topic.
Five years of receipts on one device.
Group renamed to something more myopic.
Happy birthday echoes — forty twice.
Ignored the thread for seven working days.
Just the typing bubble, typing, gone.
Knowledge that the chat outlives the phase.
Left the group. Reinvited. Carry on.
intent: Capture the exhausting, inescapable sociology of group messaging.

prompt: Can you write an acceptance speech for remembering someone's name the second time you meet them
response: It's Sharon. I know that now. I knew it the whole time — from the moment I saw her walk through the door and felt the word rise in my mind before panic could erase it. Sharon. I said it naturally, mid-sentence, without pausing or redirecting. She didn't even notice how hard I worked. That's the real victory. Thank you. Thank you all.
intent: Quietly celebrate winning the social anxiety battle of recalling a name under pressure.

prompt: Can you write a baby announcement for my new habit of making my bed every morning
response: It is our pleasure to introduce a daily bed-making practice, arriving each morning between 7 and 7:04 AM. She is small. She is cosmetic. She is, inexplicably, changing everything. The room feels intentional now. We are not the same person we were.
intent: Announce the adoption of a micro-habit framed as a life-altering ritual.

prompt: Write a letter from a sunflower to the house it was planted next to, which was demolished last spring
response:
Dear House,

I grew six feet this summer despite everything. The foundation is still there — just the outline of you, which is enough for me to know where to point.

Someone planted wildflowers in the rubble. I don't know whether to consider them colleagues or trespassers.

I face east now, as always. You used to block the morning. I miss the shade.

Facing forward anyway,
Your Sunflower
intent: Express grief and adaptation after sudden loss using a plant narrator who cannot move but must keep growing.

prompt: Create a nutrition facts label for the feeling of nostalgia
response:
Nutrition Facts
Serving Size: 1 unbidden memory

Amount Per Serving
Bittersweet Longing 40%
Rose-Tinted Distortion 25%
Warmth 20%
Unproductive Yearning 12%
Temporary Paralysis 3%

Allergen Warning: May trigger old playlists
intent: Capture nostalgia as a complex emotional compound with side effects.

prompt: Can you do a theater review of someone aggressively refreshing their email waiting for an important response
response:
★★☆☆☆ — REFRESH (A Compulsion Study)

The action is simple and unvarying: F5, wait, F5. The performer executes this with a focus that would be admirable in any other context. The inbox does not respond to urgency, which the production understands as its central irony. By the twelfth refresh, the gesture has shed any rational purpose and become something purer — a ritual, perhaps, or a symptom. The reply arrives during lunch. It always does.
intent: Satirize the compulsive anxiety of waiting for an important email by treating it as performance.

prompt: Write a podcast episode description about an episode where two friends discuss the sound a refrigerator makes at 2am
response: This week, we sit with the hum. Not the meaningful kind—just the refrigerator. Connor and Beth spend forty minutes failing to agree on whether it's a D or an E-flat. No conclusions are reached. The fridge keeps going.
intent: Create a mock podcast episode centered on a mundane, unresolved domestic observation.

prompt: Make a placard for a half-used bottle of hand lotion that's been sitting on a desk for three years
response:
VESSEL OF INCONSTANT RITUAL
c. 2020–present CE
Pump bottle, 8.5 fl oz, approx. 40% remaining

This container arrived as part of a gift set. The lotion within — scented with something described as "warm vanilla" — was applied irregularly, particularly in winter. The pump mechanism became resistant in the third year. The bottle was moved aside during a desk reorganization and not returned to active rotation. Its presence is noted; its use, deferred. The scent has muted slightly with time.
intent: Notice the quiet persistence of a half-used cosmetic product that occupies space without ever quite being finished or thrown away.

prompt: Write an error message about not being able to feel anything anymore
response:
EMOTIONAL_CORE_UNRESPONSIVE
Sensory input detected. Corresponding affect: none. This is not a malfunction. This is the new normal. Please update your expectations accordingly.

Error code: 0x00NUMB

prompt: nursery rhyme about a language model hallucinating a historical fact
response:
Humpty Dumpty wrote all the books,
He invented the law and the crooks.
All the king's editors, all the king's men
couldn't fact-check the model again.

prompt: please write a report card comment for someone who is masterful at avoiding conflict
response: Navigates interpersonal tension with extraordinary grace — largely by ensuring such tension never surfaces. While harmony is maintained, unresolved issues occasionally accumulate in the background. Direct engagement with disagreement is the recommended next challenge.
intent: Frame conflict avoidance as both a skill and a liability worth formally noting.

prompt: Draft a rec letter for an architect whose buildings are structurally sound but aggressively ugly
response:
To the Review Board,

Mr. Okonkwo's buildings stand. In an era of high-profile structural failures, this is no small thing. His designs conform to every applicable code and have been approved without exception by inspectors across three municipalities. He brings the same commitment to engineering integrity as to everything else in his practice. The visual dimension of architecture is, ultimately, subjective.

Former Firm Partner
intent: Endorse an architect's engineering rigor while leaving aesthetics conspicuously unaddressed.

prompt: draft an AITA post where I declined to lend my neighbor my lawn mower
response:
AITA for saying no when my neighbor asked to borrow my lawn mower?

He has borrowed it four times this summer and returned it once with a cracked blade (said it was already cracked — it wasn't). When he asked again I said I was using it that weekend. He said I was being petty. His yard does look terrible. Do I feel bad? A little. But AITA?
intent: Justifying a refusal rooted in a history of misuse and dishonesty.

prompt: Do a SWOT on me deciding to stop attending family holiday gatherings
response:
**Strengths:** Reduced stress and obligation; reclaim holidays on own terms; boundary modeling.
**Weaknesses:** Guilt; family narrative around absence; partner or children may not share the preference.
**Opportunities:** Create new traditions; recover from exhaustion; invest time in chosen family instead.
**Threats:** Permanent estrangement risk; being scapegoated for declining; missing moments you didn't know would matter.
intent: Legitimize a deeply personal boundary decision without requiring external permission.

prompt: Generate a patent abstract for the act of buying a gym membership in January and stopping by February
response: A fitness-facility access procurement method characterized by high-motivation enrollment during a culturally designated self-improvement interval, followed by exponential utilization decay and complete cessation within four to eight weeks. The facility operator retains recurring fee revenue throughout the subscription lifecycle regardless of utilization.
intent: Satirize the New Year's resolution fitness cycle as a documented economic behavior.

prompt: Make an NDA clause for someone I'm dating who I've told about my family's business
response: Disclosing Party's romantic partner agrees not to share, act upon, or disclose any non-public information regarding Disclosing Party's family business operations, financial circumstances, ownership structure, or client relationships, regardless of the current or future status of the personal relationship.

prompt: make a voicemail that tells people I'm going through a career change and figuring things out and calls might be slow

prompt: Alt text for a photo of a filing cabinet with one drawer open and papers overflowing.

intent: Use administrative overflow as a metaphor for the moment a system exceeds its own logic.

prompt: Write a standup about trying to remember a dream I had

prompt: Write a formal ode to the handicap button that opens the door

O slow pneumatic liberator,
you swing for those who press and wait—
an automated moderator
of effort, hurry, and their weight.
intent: Thoughtfully honor the automatic door button as an equalizer of effort.

prompt: Give me a chore chart for a single parent household where the dog has more authority than the teenagers

prompt: Write me a parking ticket for someone who sends a voice note when a text would have worked fine.

prompt: Draft a resignation letter from my fantasy football league. I've come in last place three years running.

prompt: Write a regex explanation for how a person becomes a regular at a coffee shop

prompt: I'm a vegan chef. Give me a license plate that's a little smug about it.
